title: Rosetta stone for database inputs reveals serious security issue
abstract:
We introduce the first method for translating text embeddings from one vector space to another without any paired data, encoders, or predefined sets of matches. Our unsupervised approach translates any embedding to and from a universal latent representation (i.e., a universal semantic structure conjectured by the Platonic Representation Hypothesis). Our translations achieve high cosine similarity across model pairs with different architectures, parameter counts, and training datasets. The ability to translate unknown embeddings into a different space while preserving their geometry has serious implications for security. An adversary with access to a database of only embedding vectors can extract sensitive information about underlying documents, sufficient for classification and attribute inference.

Figure 1: Left: input embeddings from different model families (T5-based GTR [46] and BERT-based GTE [32]) are fundamentally incomparable. Right: given unpaired embedding samples from different models on different texts, our model learns a latent representation where they are closely aligned.
introduction:
Introduction

Text embeddings are the backbone of modern NLP, powering tasks like retrieval, RAG, classification, and clustering. There are many embedding models trained on different datasets, data shufflings, and initializations. An embedding of a text encodes its semantics: a good model maps texts with similar semantics to vectors close to each other in the embedding space. Since semantics is a property of text, different embeddings of the same text should encode the same semantics. In practice, however, different models encode texts into completely different and incompatible vector spaces.

The Platonic Representation Hypothesis [18] conjectures that all image models of sufficient size converge to the same latent representation. We propose a stronger, constructive version of this hypothesis for text models: the universal latent structure of text representations can be learned and, furthermore, harnessed to translate representations from one space to another without any paired data or encoders.

Figure 2: Given only a vector database from an unknown model, vec2vec translates the database into the space of a known model using latent structure alone. Converted embeddings reveal sensitive information about the original documents, such as the topic of an email (pictured, real example).

In this work, we show that the Strong Platonic Representation Hypothesis holds in practice. Given unpaired examples of embeddings from two models with different architectures and training data, our method learns a latent representation in which the embeddings are almost identical (Figure 1).

We draw inspiration from research on aligning word embeddings across languages [61, 10, 15, 9] and unsupervised image translation [35, 68]. Our vec2vec method uses adversarial losses and cycle consistency to learn to encode embeddings into a shared latent space and decode with minimal loss. This makes unsupervised translation possible. We use a basic adversarial approach with vector space preservation [45] to learn a mapping from an unknown embedding distribution to a known one.

vec2vec is the first method to successfully translate embeddings from the space of one model to another without paired data.1 vec2vec translations achieve cosine similarity as high as 0.92 to the ground-truth vectors in their target embedding spaces and perfect matching on over 8000 shuffled embeddings (without access to the set of possible matches in advance).

To show that our translations preserve not only the relative geometry of embeddings but also the semantics of underlying inputs, we extract information from them using zero-shot attribute inference and inversion, without any knowledge of the model that produced the original embeddings.2
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline:  
Unsupervised Embedding Translation Unveils Security Risks Under the Platonic Representation Hypothesis

News Article:  
A recently published arXiv paper, “Harnessing the Universal Geometry of Embeddings,” has ignited urgent discussion within the research community and technical press alike. Drawing coverage from specialist outlets—one notably describing the development as a “‘Rosetta stone’ for database inputs” that “reveals serious security issue”—the work presents an unsupervised methodology for translating text embeddings between vector spaces without paired data, encoders, or predefined matches [Real-World Anchor Event].

Text embeddings serve as the core representational backbone of modern natural language processing (NLP) systems, facilitating operations such as retrieval, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), classification, and clustering. Although multiple text embedding models exist, each trained on varying corpora, architectures, and initialization procedures, their resulting embedding spaces are typically considered incompatible. The technical consensus, until now, has been that the geometry and semantics captured by these diverse embeddings—while purporting to encode text meaning—are fundamentally model-specific and not directly interoperable.

This assumption is directly challenged by the paper’s constructive extension of the Platonic Representation Hypothesis. The hypothesis conjectures that all high-capacity models ultimately converge on a universal latent space; the present work demonstrates, for the first time, that this universal latent structure for text embeddings can not only be learned but operationalized for cross-space translation. The proposed vec2vec method leverages unsupervised learning—absent any paired embeddings or supervision—to encode input vectors from one model into a shared latent space and subsequently decode them into the embedding space of another model, regardless of architectural divergences or training data heterogeneity [Abstract, Introduction].

In concrete terms, vec2vec utilizes adversarial losses and cycle consistency constraints, aligning with methodological precedents from unsupervised image translation and adversarial word embedding mapping. By training on unpaired samples from distinct models and texts, the procedure learns a latent manifold that harmonizes geometrical and semantic correspondences between embedding spaces, independent of any explicit alignment or supervised signal [Introduction].

Empirical results underscore the technical breakthrough: translations performed by vec2vec achieve high cosine similarity—up to 0.92 relative to ground-truth target vectors—across diverse model pairs, including those with disjoint architectures (e.g., T5-based GTR versus BERT-based GTE). The approach demonstrates perfect recovery of shuffling correspondences on over 8000 embedding pairs, despite the absence of prior knowledge of the matchings or the structural details of the underlying models [Abstract, Introduction].

Perhaps most significantly from a systems and security research perspective, the authors establish that the semantic and geometric integrity of embeddings is preserved under translation. The implications are highly consequential: with only the embedding vectors from an unknown database, it becomes feasible to reconstruct sensitive information about the original documents—enabling zero-shot attribute inference, classification, and inversion attacks, even without any knowledge of the generating model or the input texts themselves [Abstract, Figure 2]. This finding foregrounds the emerging security risk: vector databases, widely used under the presumption of semantic opacity, may in fact be vulnerable to adversarial extraction of private or proprietary content.

The paper positions vec2vec as the first method to enable translation of text embeddings from one model’s space to another without supervised alignment of any kind. Importantly, the authors tie their results to the Strong Platonic Representation Hypothesis, rigorously operationalizing the claim that universal latent geometry is both discoverable and translatable. This work thus calls for a fundamental re-evaluation of the security assumptions underlying vector-based retrieval, data storage, and model-agnostic knowledge transfer.

Given the profound implications for both the theoretical understanding of model representation and the practical risks to data confidentiality, stakeholders across NLP, security, and database engineering are urged to interrogate the details of this new methodology. To critically assess the technical innovations and the attendant security vulnerabilities, access the full paper at https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.12540.

anchor_event.full_content:
A breakthrough perspective comes from the recent arXiv paper “Harnessing the Universal Geometry of Embeddings”.This paper introduces a new, unsupervised method for translating text embeddings from one vector space to another without needing any paired data, encoders, or predefined matches between the spaces. The approach is based on the idea that there exists a universal latent representation underlying all text embeddings—a concept the authors call the "Platonic Representation Hypothesis."

This paper introduces a novel unsupervised method, vec2vec, for translating text embeddings between different vector spaces without requiring any paired data, encoders, or predefined matches. This breakthrough suggests the existence of a universal latent representation or a universal semantic structure that all large language models converge towards, supporting what the authors call the Platonic Representation Hypothesis.

*   Unsupervised Translation of Embeddings: The core innovation is the ability to translate text embeddings from one model's vector space to another, even if the models have different architectures, parameter counts, or training datasets. This is achieved by mapping any embedding to and from a universal latent representation.
*   High Cosine Similarity: The translations achieved high cosine similarity across various model pairs, indicating that the semantic relationships between vectors are preserved during the translation process.
*   Implications for Security: This research has significant implications for the security of vector databases. It suggests that an adversary with access only to embedding vectors could potentially extract sensitive information about the underlying documents, sufficient for classification and attribute inference, even without direct access to the original text or models.

title: New framework helps AI systems recover from mistakes and find optimal solutions
abstract: We introduce a new approach to agent programming, the development of LLM- based agents. Current approaches to agent programming often entangle two aspects of agent design: the core workflow logic and the inference-time strategy (e.g., tree search). We introduce probabilistic angelic nondeterminism (PAN), a programming model that disentangles these two concerns, allowing the programmer to describe the agent workflow and independently experiment with different inference-time strategies by simply changing a few inputs. We provide an implementation of PAN in Python as the ENCOMPASS framework, which uses a Python decorator to compile agent workflow programs into a search space. We present three case studies that demonstrate how the framework lets the programmer quickly improve the reliability of an agent and easily switch between different inference-time strategies, all with little additional coding.
introduction:
Introduction

Recent work has shown the power of scaling inference-time compute for LLMs [1, 2], where popular strategies include best-of-N sampling [3, 4, 5], refinement [6, 7], and tree search [8, 9]. In LLM-based agents — systems that define how LLMs and other components interact to solve a task — these same strategies have become common ways of improving performance and reliability. Furthermore, several works have demonstrated the utility of applying sophisticated search and backtracking strategies in AI agents to improve performance in various tasks [8, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14].

While various frameworks have been developed to simplify the low-level interaction between the program and the LLM [15, 16, 17, 18], a framework for agent inference-time strategies has been absent. Our goal is to develop an inference-time strategy framework: a framework that makes it easy to experiment with different inference-time strategies independently of the design and implementation of the underlying agent workflow. Such a framework is intended not to replace, but to be used in conjunction with LLM prompting and tool use frameworks, such as LangChain [15] or DSPy [16].

We target “program-in-control” style agents, where one defines the workflow in code and uses the LLM to accomplish specific subtasks [14, 19, 20, 21, 22].2 In these agents, inference-time strategies have traditionally been limited to sampling and refinement loops [6, 7, 19, 20], whereas more sophisticated strategies such as beam search and tree search have been rarely explored [14].

∗Work performed as a consultant for Asari AI 2This “program-in-control” style contrasts with the “LLM-in-control” style where the LLM decides the full

sequence of operations (tool calls) in the workflow [8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13].

Figure 1: An ENCOMPASS program specifies an agent workflow, which is compiled into a search space object, and inference-time scaling is accomplished through search over the nondeterministic execution paths of the agent workflow.

We identify the key bottleneck to be the entanglement of the inference-time scaling strategy with the core workflow logic when programming the agent. Programmers typically bake the inference- time strategy into the agent workflow [14, 19, 20, 22], which is inflexible, reduces readability, and limits the kinds of inference-time strategies that can be easily implemented. Therefore, we aim to design a framework that cleanly separates the representation of the core workflow logic from the inference-time scaling strategy. The programmer could then make minimal modifications to their agent to flexibly experiment with different inference-time strategies. Also, different agents would no longer require custom implementations of the same inference-time strategy, but can instead reuse a common implementation.

Our key insight is that inference-time strategies can be viewed as instances of search over different execution paths of a nondeterministic program. We developed the ENCOMPASS Python programming framework (“enhancing agents with compiled agent search”), depicted in Figure 1. Figure 1a and Figure 1b show an agent program and its corresponding workflow, respectively. The user specifies the “locations of unreliability” in their agent source code using branchpoint() statements. A location of unreliability is an operation such as an LLM call where repeated invocations produce outputs of varying quality. Since these different outputs give rise to multiple possible futures of the program’s execution, the program has a tree of possible execution paths. ENCOMPASS compiles the program into a search space object (Figure 1c) so that search can be conducted over this tree of execution paths to find the path with the highest score (Figure 1d). We call this programming model probabilistic angelic nondeterminism (PAN). As a form of angelic nondeterminism [23], PAN lets the programmer write their program pretending the unreliable operations always produce good outputs, and the runtime searches the space of possible execution paths for one where the operations indeed produced good outputs.

Our work makes the following concrete contributions:

We introduce the PAN programming model (Section 2.1), which uses angelic nondetermin- ism to separate inference-time algorithms (search policy) from the underlying logic of the agent (specification of the search space).

We present ENCOMPASS, a Python library that implements PAN (Section 2.2), providing 1. primitives like branchpoint() that the programmer can use inside their ENCOMPASS function, 2. a Python function decorator that compiles an ENCOMPASS function into a search space object at run-time, and 3. common search algorithms, as well as an interface for implementing custom search algorithms.

We illustrate how ENCOMPASS provides a unifying framework for common inference-time strategies and agentic patterns, which are special cases of search over nondeterministic execution paths of ENCOMPASS programs (Section 3). ENCOMPASS also provides a natural generalization of these inference-time strategies.

We present case studies showing how ENCOMPASS enables easy experimentation of various inference-time search strategies over an underlying agent workflow, allowing one to quickly identify the best-performing strategy (Section 4). ENCOMPASS opens up new possibilities for inference-time scaling of program-in-control style agents, where inference-time strategies that were previously considered too cumbersome to implement are now made possible by ENCOMPASS.
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline: ENCOMPASS Framework Formalizes Inference-Time Search Strategies in LLM-Based Agents

News Article:

A new programming model for LLM-based agents, ENCOMPASS, is garnering attention following coverage in MIT News for its principled approach to disentangling agent workflow logic from inference-time strategy. The framework, developed by researchers at MIT and Asari AI, introduces probabilistic angelic nondeterminism (PAN) as its programming paradigm, enabling a clear separation between the specification of an agent's logic and the implementation of search-based inference-time scaling strategies ([Real-World Anchor Event]).

The technical motivation for ENCOMPASS arises from a fundamental challenge in agentic AI systems: workflow logic and inference-time search strategies are typically interwoven. Current approaches to agent programming require developers to couple the definition of task workflows and search policies such as best-of-N sampling, refinement loops, and tree search, with each new agent or strategy often necessitating bespoke, hard-coded implementations ([Abstract], [Introduction]). This entanglement constrains systematic experimentation with search strategies and impedes agent reliability and generalizability.

ENCOMPASS addresses this bottleneck by introducing PAN, which leverages the notion of angelic nondeterminism—a concept wherein program execution presumes that nondeterministic (i.e., unreliable or stochastic) steps will yield optimal outputs if such a path exists ([Abstract], [Introduction]). In the PAN model, inference-time strategies are formalized as instances of search over nondeterministic execution paths, shifting the inference-time logic from low-level agent code to configurable, reusable search procedures.

Practically, ENCOMPASS is realized as a Python library, which uses a decorator to compile agent workflow programs into a search space object. Programmers annotate their workflow code by marking "locations of unreliability"—typically LLM invocations—via `branchpoint()` primitives. During execution, ENCOMPASS constructs a tree of possible states stemming from these nondeterministic choices. Search algorithms can then be applied over this tree to identify execution paths that maximize a scoring metric. The core agent workflow thus remains invariant, while inference-time policies—such as backtracking, best-first search, or custom tree search—can be interchanged by adjusting arguments to the search overlay ([Abstract], [Introduction]).

This abstraction yields salient implications for the development and scaling of program-in-control style LLM agents—those in which the workflow is explicitly encoded and the LLM is invoked to perform discrete subtasks. The separation of concerns allows the programmer to rapidly iterate on inference-time strategies without altering the workflow logic itself and to systematically compare agent reliability across strategies. Notably, prior frameworks have focused almost exclusively on integrating LLMs and programmatic workflows at the prompt or tool-use level ([Introduction]). ENCOMPASS generalizes and unifies these patterns by making search over nondeterministic executions a first-class construct.

In three case studies presented in the research, ENCOMPASS demonstrates its capacity to improve agent reliability and enable straightforward experimentation across a variety of inference-time search strategies with minimal code modifications. The framework supports an extensible interface for custom search algorithms and exposes primitives for scoring and backtracking. By compiling agent source code into an explicit search space representation, ENCOMPASS decouples inference-time compute scaling (e.g., number of search samples) from workflow specification—facilitating not just replication but genuine research into optimal search policies for agent performance ([Abstract]).

The contextual relevance of ENCOMPASS is underscored by increasing interest in structured search methodologies for LLM-based code generation, analysis, and translation workflows. As highlighted in MIT News, Professor Yiming Yang of Carnegie Mellon University notes that ENCOMPASS "offers a principled way to explore how structured search can enhance code generation, translation, and analysis," while lead author Zhening Li emphasizes the power of cleanly separating agent programming logic from search strategies ([Real-World Anchor Event]).

It is critical to note the targeted scope of this approach: ENCOMPASS is specifically designed for "program-in-control" agents, in contrast to "LLM-in-control" architectures where the LLM dictates the sequence of operations. In the latter paradigm, where there is no explicit programmatic workflow to optimize over, the utility of PAN and ENCOMPASS is substantially reduced ([Real-World Anchor Event]).

The ENCOMPASS framework thus establishes a formal, reusable infrastructure for agentic search-driven reliability improvements in LLM-based agents. Its technical merit lies in generalizing inference-time search as a modular, programmable component, providing researchers with a foundation for systematic comparison and composition of search strategies.

Researchers and developers engaged in agent programming are encouraged to consult the full research paper ([Citation]) for detailed implementation guidance, proofs of concept, and further case studies. ENCOMPASS opens new avenues for exploration in the principled design and scaling of search-driven agent systems within the Python ecosystem.

anchor_event.summary: ENCOMPASS is featured in multiple high-quality sources, notably in MIT News ([0]) and tech publications ([3]). MIT News in particular highlights ENCOMPASS's ability to backtrack and optimize AI agent search trajectories, which illustrates real-world impact and public recognition for the approach introduced in the research paper. This coverage provides a credible and technical hook, validating ENCOMPASS's novelty and application in agent reliability and scalability.
anchor_event.full_content:
“EnCompass arrives at a timely moment, as AI-driven agents and search-based techniques are beginning to reshape workflows in software engineering,” says Carnegie Mellon University Professor Yiming Yang, who wasn’t involved in the research. “By cleanly separating an agent’s programming logic from its inference-time search strategy, the framework offers a principled way to explore how structured search can enhance code generation, translation, and analysis. This abstraction provides a solid foundation for more systematic and reliable search-driven approaches to software development.”

“With EnCompass, we’ve separated the search strategy from the underlying workflow of an AI agent,” says lead author Zhening Li ’25, MEng ’25, who is an MIT electrical engineering and computer science (EECS) PhD student, CSAIL researcher, and research consultant at Asari AI. “Our framework lets programmers easily experiment with different search strategies to find the one that makes the AI agent perform the best.”

The researchers add that EnCompass targets agents where a program specifies the steps of the high-level workflow; the current iteration of their framework is less applicable to agents that are entirely controlled by an LLM. “In those agents, instead of having a program that specifies the steps and then using an LLM to carry out those steps, the LLM itself decides everything,” says Li. “There is no underlying programmatic workflow, so you can execute inference-time search on whatever the LLM invents on the fly. In this case, there’s less need for a tool like EnCompass that modifies how a program executes with search and backtracking.”

title: Digital technique puts rendered fabric in the best light
abstract: Realistic fabric rendering is still a significant challenge due to their complex structures and varying fiber properties. We present a new fabric shading technique, which models both reflection and transmission using a hybrid of ray and wave optics methods, grounded in simulation data. We target fabrics woven from yarns, each formed by twisting together one or more plies, which further contain twisted fibers. Our model is based on simulations that predict the scattering of a narrow Gaussian beam by a single ply. Comparing results from full-wave simulations and path tracing, we found that ray optics can accurately simulate the average far field scattering from an ensemble of plies, but not the variation among individual ply instances, and ray tracing overlooks important diffraction effects. Following these observations, our model is built from ray simulations performed for many ply instances, with simulation data fitted by Gaussian mixtures to be used during rendering. Wave simulations are used to calibrate noise functions that account for instance-to-instance variation, and an aperture diffraction model is used to handle light passing between plies and yarns. The result is a hybrid model capable of producing realistic appearance and highlight structure in fabrics, while capturing spatial break-ups and irregularities and simulating the subtle color shifts and blurriness that occur in transmission. We validate our results by comparing rendered images with photographs, demonstrating the effectiveness of our approach in achieving realistic cloth rendering.
introduction: In recent years, significant progress has been made in fabric render-ing, particularly through ply-based models [Zhu et al. 2024, 2023a]. These models have substantially improved realism by relying onphysical principles and measurable properties, rather than on em-pirical methods [Estevez and Kulla 2017; Zeltner et al . 2022] thatmatch appearance purely through observation.Despite these advancements, faithfully capturing the appearanceof fabrics remains challenging. Modeling both reflection and trans-mission accurately is difficult, and characteristic irregularities ofreal materials are often inadequately addressed. Additionally, manycurrent models rely on idealized assumptions—for instance, fibersare assumed to be perfectly circular and lack natural variation, andwave optics effects are ignored—which do not reflect the complexityof real-world fabrics.We propose a ply model derived from first principles, combiningthe realism of wave optics with the speed of ray optics.  Our ply scattering model is based on multi-fiber full-wave simulations andpath tracing, where both simulation methods account for the true,generalized shapes of fibers. Comparisons between wave and raysimulations show that ray optics can accurately compute the aver-age far field scattering from a large collection of plies, but not thevariation among individual ply instances. Thus, we model averageply scattering by fitting ray simulation results to a small set of Gauss-ian lobes, achieving a lightweight representation. Moreover, sincethe spatially varying and sometimes glinty appearance of fabricsarises from instance-to-instance differences in ply structures, wederive noise data from a limited set of wave simulations and usethem to augment our average ply scattering model.Moreover, our model handles multi-bounce interactions betweenmultiple plies and in double-yarn configurations, resulting in a new,more diffuse transmission model that captures complex scatteringand absorption effects across ply layers. Additionally, we introducewave optics based diffraction lobes to more realistically model trans-mission through gaps and around yarn edges. Together, these com-ponents enable a robust and accurate approach to fabric rendering,contributing to the advancement of appearance modeling.
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline:  
Hybrid Wave-Ray Model Marks Milestone in Realistic Fabric Rendering

News Article:

In a noteworthy advance for digital material appearance, a new research collaboration between Cornell University and NVIDIA has established the first fabric rendering model explicitly incorporating wave optics effects, surpassing previous reliance on purely empirical or ray-based approaches. This hybrid method, grounded in recent results published in ACM Transactions on Graphics [doi:10.1145/3763359], addresses the technical challenge of simulating both complex reflection and transmission—crucial for materials whose visual richness stems from the intricate structure and variability intrinsic to woven fabrics.

The research responds directly to the growing need in computer graphics for models that reproduce the nuanced behavior of light as it interacts with textile structures. Earlier attempts to leverage wave optics alone proved computationally infeasible for realistic, interactive image synthesis. As described in the anchor event, the investigators found that whereas ray optics methods efficiently capture average far-field scattering—accounting for overall highlight and color—they miss critical spatial variations, glints, and transmission blurriness arising from optical phenomena such as diffraction. The solution: a hybrid pipeline, integrating the complementary strengths of ray and wave optics.

At its core, the proposed model operates on a physically grounded ply-level representation: fabrics are abstracted as collections of yarns, each itself a bundle of twisted plies, with each ply comprising multiple twisted fibers. The researchers conduct comprehensive full-wave and ray simulations to characterize the scattering response of single plies to a narrow Gaussian light beam. The results reveal that while ray optics accurately estimate average scattering for large ensembles of plies, wave-based effects are required to capture statistical variability and local highlight structure—details critical for realism.

Concretely, the technique proceeds by fitting results from ray simulations of numerous ply instances to Gaussian mixtures that represent average ply scattering within the model. To encode the spatially varying appearance and glinty effects seen in real fabrics, a limited cohort of full-wave simulations informs a set of calibrated noise functions, which modulate the scattering lobes to simulate instance-to-instance variation. In addition, an aperture diffraction model is incorporated at the yarn and ply level, enabling rendering of light transmission through gaps and around edges, thus capturing blurring, subtle color shifts, and complex transmission scatter not resolvable by ray tracing alone.

A further innovation lies in the treatment of multi-bounce light transport, particularly for dense weave configurations where inter-ply and multi-yarn interactions result in significant diffusion and absorption. The proposed model enables new, more diffuse transmission representations that more faithfully mirror photographs of real cloth, outperforming prior empirical and idealized analytic approaches.

In practical terms, this hybrid wave-ray model enables physically accurate rendering of fabric appearance—including highlight structure, spatial break-ups, color shifts, and blurring arising from real-world textile geometry—with computational efficiency compatible with modern rendering frameworks. Its modularity and scalability open the door for its application in film production, interactive graphics, and virtual fashion. Here, physically plausible digital cloth offers foundational improvements in visual credibility, directly benefiting digital wardrobe creation, realistic costume design, and virtual fitting rooms where fidelity to real-world material cues is paramount.

The model, as presented, represents a significant technical progression in appearance modeling, demonstrating measurable agreement with photographic reference data—an important validation step in computer graphics methodology. By bridging the speed of ray-based techniques with wave optics accuracy for localized effects, the research sets a precedent for hybrid models in other material systems where iridescence, microstructure, and subsurface scattering play defining visual roles.

Researchers, developers, and practitioners seeking to advance physically based appearance models or integrate next-generation fabric rendering into production pipelines are encouraged to consult the full technical report and implementation details via ACM [https://dl.acm.org/doi/epdf/10.1145/3763359].

anchor_event.full_content:
At first, she tried to model the fabric’s appearance entirely using wave optics, but the simulation was too computationally intensive. Then, she discovered that using ray optics, which is about 1,000 times faster, works well for generating the average color of the fabric and look of the highlights. She could then save the slower wave-optics simulations to render the light shining through the fabric from behind, and for the subtle glints, sparkles and imperfections that make the images look especially realistic.

In the new study with Andrea Weidlich, a principal researcher at NVIDIA, Yu went further by considering how light interacts with cloth, both as a ray and a wave. She modeled light rays that bounce off the fibers – similar to Irawan’s initial model – and light waves that bend and diffract as they pass through gaps between fibers. This was the first fabric model to take wave optics into account, and built off her recent work[rendering iridescent feathers](https://news.cornell.edu/stories/2025/01/researchers-put-shine-digitally-rendered-feathers).

Now, Cornell researchers, in partnership with the technology company NVIDIA, have developed a method for creating digital images of cloth that more accurately captures the texture of textiles.

title: From brain scans to alloys Teaching AI to make sense of complex research data
abstract: Traditional entropy-based methods—such as cross-entropy loss in classification problems—have long been essential tools for representing the information uncertainty and physical disorder in data and for developing artificial intelligence algorithms. However, the rapid growth of data across various domains has introduced new challenges, particularly the integration of heterogeneous datasets with intrinsic disparities. To address this, we introduce a zentropy-enhanced neural network (ZENN), extending zentropy theory into the data science domain via intrinsic entropy, enabling more effective learning from heterogeneous data sources. ZENN simultaneously learns both energy and intrinsic entropy components, capturing the underlying structure of multisource data. To support this, we redesign the neural network architecture to better reflect the intrinsic properties and variability inherent in diverse datasets. We demonstrate the effectiveness of ZENN on classification tasks and energy landscape reconstructions, showing its superior generalization capabilities and robustness-particularly in predicting high-order derivatives. In image and text classification tasks, ZENN demonstrates superior generalization by introducing a learnable temperature variable that models latent multisource heterogeneity, allowing it to surpass state-of-the-art models on CIFAR-10/100, BBC News, and AG News. As a practical application in materials science, we employ ZENN to reconstruct the Helmholtz energy landscape of Fe3Pt using data generated from density functional theory and capture key material behaviors, including negative thermal expansion and the critical point in the temperature–pressure space. Overall, this work presents a zentropy-grounded framework for data-driven machine learning, positioning ZENN as a versatile and robust approach for scientific problems involving complex, heterogeneous datasets.
introduction: The rise of complex, heterogeneous datasets is transforming data science, enabling new capabilities across fields such as biomedical modeling, materials discovery, climate prediction, and the development of digital twins. These digital replicas of physical systems rely on the seamless integration of real-time and historical data from multiple sources, including sensors, machines, experiments, and simulations (1–3). As a powerful paradigm for empirical discovery, data-driven modeling has been widely applied to solve inverse problems in physics (4), uncover biological mechanisms (5–7), and enable precision diagnostics for complex diseases (8, 9). A key tool in these approaches is entropy, a fundamental concept in thermodynamics and information theory. Entropy provides a framework for modeling uncertainty, disorder, and irreversibility across a wide range of systems, from black holes (10, 11) and ecosystems (12) to digital infrastructures and quantum materials (13–15). According to the second law of thermodynamics, internal processes in closed systems are irreversible, leading to positive entropy production (12, 16). Thermodynamic principles have increasingly influenced deep learning: for instance, neural networks have been developed to construct DFTbased potential energy surfaces (17), Boltzmann Generators embed Boltzmann entropy to predict protein structures (18), and entropy-based frameworks like Thermodynamics-Inspired Explainable Representations and EPR-Net improve interpretability and modeling of high-dimensional non-equilibrium systems (19–21). Entropy-driven objectives—such as cross-entropy loss—have been widely applied in machine learning(22–25), particularly for classification tasks (26). However, traditional entropy-based methods typically provide only macroscopic or microscopic approximations and often fail to account for internal disparities when fusing multisource data. These limitations are particularly pronounced for real-world datasets, which vary in scale, resolution, measurement conditions, and statistical properties—posing challenges for conventional machine learning methods that assume data homogeneity and stationarity (27). To overcome these challenges, zentropy theory introduces a thermodynamically consistent framework that incorporates intrinsic entropy, capturing variability across datasets due to environmental, experimental, or contextual differences (28, 29). Originally developed to predict magnetic phase diagrams in fcc-Ce (30) and L12-Fe3Pt (31), zentropy has since been applied to a wide range of materials, including strongly correlated systems (32–34), liquid and melting phases (35), plasticity (36), and superconductivity (37). Despite its success in physics, zentropy’s reliance on DFT for computing configuration-level entropy has limited its application in broader data-driven domains. Nevertheless, as a foundational framework for integrating heterogeneous data, zentropy remains essential for ensuring thermodynamic consistency and enabling more accurate, comprehensive models. Building on this foundation, we introduce a zentropyenhanced neural network (ZENN) that embeds zentropy theory into deep learning to enable entropy-aware learning from complex, heterogeneous datasets. Unlike Shannon entropy, zentropy’s thermodynamic basis captures domainspecific complexity, providing richer structure for modeling multi-source data. By incorporating zentropy into neural network design, ZENN learns both energy and intrinsic entropy components, capturing the underlying structure of heterogeneous datasets. This approach extends the applicability of zentropy beyond quantum and statistical mechanics, offering a robust framework for integrating heterogeneous, real-world data and improving predictive performance in classification, energy landscape reconstruction, and other scientific applications. While zentropy has been widely used in forward modeling, where it plays a critical role in predicting system behavior, its integration into data-driven approaches for inverse problems remains unexplored. This paper addresses this gap by embedding zentropy theory into data science, advancing the application of entropy-based modeling within the context of data-driven techniques. More specifically, to broaden the applicability of zentropy theory beyond its traditional domain, we developed ZENN—a framework designed to integrate multi-source data, reconstruct energy landscapes, and infer system properties. We benchmarked ZENN against cross-entropy loss using state-of-the-art models for both image and text classification tasks and energy landscape reconstruction. In all cases, ZENN consistently outperformed cross-entropy loss in terms of accuracy and stability. To further validate its capabilities, ZENN was applied to the Fe3Pt compound, successfully capturing its negative thermal expansion (NTE) behavior and accurately predicting its critical point in the temperature-pressure/volume spaces. These results demonstrate ZENN’s strong generalization ability and its enhanced performance in estimating highorder derivatives—an essential feature for modeling complex thermodynamic systems
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline: ZENN: Zentropy-Enhanced Neural Network Redefines Learning from Heterogeneous Data

News Article:  
The growing need to analyze and integrate complex, heterogeneous datasets—a challenge spanning fields from materials design to the development of digital twins—has driven researchers to seek more rigorous frameworks for data-driven modeling. This imperative is underscored by the rapid acceptance of the Zentropy-Enhanced Neural Network (ZENN) framework, recently published in PNAS and highlighted by independent sources for its superior performance over incumbent methods, notably cross-entropy, across standard classification benchmarks and scientific modeling tasks [Real-World Anchor Event].

Traditional machine learning frameworks, particularly those reliant on entropy-based objectives like cross-entropy loss, are well-established tools for representing information uncertainty and facilitating classification. Yet, their efficacy sharply diminishes when confronted with the intrinsic disparities characterizing multi-source data—variations in scale, measurement, and underlying distributions that defy the assumption of homogeneity and stationarity often encoded in classical algorithms [Abstract, Introduction].

ZENN emerges as a substantive advance by extending zentropy theory—a thermodynamically consistent approach initially developed for modeling magnetic phase diagrams and complex material systems—into the broader domain of data science. Unlike Shannon entropy, which measures disorder at a statistical or information-theoretic level, zentropy is rooted in thermodynamic principles and captures what the authors term "intrinsic entropy": the internal variability present across heterogeneous datasets due to contextual, environmental, or experimental differences [Abstract, Introduction].

At the architectural level, ZENN distinguishes itself by simultaneously modeling both the energy and intrinsic entropy of input data, effectively embedding domain-specific complexity into the learning process. Moreover, the introduction of a learnable temperature variable allows ZENN to adapt to latent heterogeneity, providing a mechanism to capture and utilize multisource variability instead of being confounded by it [Abstract]. This innovation is particularly salient given the proliferation of complex data across biomedical simulations, climate models, sensor-driven monitoring, and elsewhere.

Empirically, ZENN’s capabilities have been validated on canonical benchmarks—CIFAR-10/100 for image classification, and BBC News and AG News for text classification—where it consistently surpassed models trained with traditional cross-entropy loss in both accuracy and robustness. Beyond classification, ZENN proved adept at reconstructing high-order features of energy landscapes in material science settings. In particular, applied to Fe₃Pt with data from density functional theory, ZENN was able to capture phenomena such as negative thermal expansion behavior and accurately forecast the critical point in temperature–pressure space—results considered significant in the study and engineering of advanced materials [Abstract, Real-World Anchor Event].

The broader implications are considerable: ZENN’s incorporation of zentropy not only enhances predictive generalization and model stability but also ensures the thermodynamic consistency of data-driven models—often a missing link when AI methods are applied to physical, biomedical, and engineered systems [Introduction]. By bridging the physical rigor of thermodynamics with deep learning, ZENN lays the foundation for more reliable scientific discovery, spanning the integration of experimental, observational, and simulated data from disparate sources [Abstract].

As scientific and engineering domains increasingly rely on multisource, real-world data, ZENN’s framework represents a notable step toward interpretable, scalable, and robust computational modeling—a bridge between the theoretical consistency demanded by physical sciences and the empirical power of modern AI. The open-source release of ZENN further signals its potential for widespread adoption and extension across scientific fields [Real-World Anchor Event].

For experts seeking the technical rigor and cross-domain potential described here, the full paper provides detailed methodological insights, empirical results, and source code access. The original research can be accessed at https://dx.doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2511227122.

Researchers, practitioners, and interdisciplinary teams are strongly encouraged to engage with the ZENN framework, assess its theoretical underpinnings, and explore its open-source implementation to address the persistent challenge of integrating and interpreting heterogeneous data in scientific and engineering contexts.

## Automated Model-Based Reinforcement Learning for Dynamic Resource Allocation in Edge Computing Environments **Abstract:** This paper introduces a novel approach to dynamic resource allocation in edge computing environments using Model-Based Reinforcement Learning (MBRL). Unlike traditional methods relying on static configurations or reactive heuristics, our system employs a learned predictive model of network state and task demands to proactively optimize resource utilization, maximizing throughput and minimizing latency. The system, termed **EDGE-MBA**, leverages a combination of Gaussian Process Regression and Deep Neural Networks to construct a versatile predictive model, enabling robust performance across diverse and fluctuating edge scenarios....

## Automated Catalyst Discovery and Optimization via Dynamic Reaction Network Analysis(DRNA) **Abstract:** This paper presents a novel framework, Dynamic Reaction Network Analysis (DRNA), for accelerating the discovery and optimization of catalysts for complex chemical reactions. DRNA leverages multi-modal data from experimental and computational sources to construct and evolve dynamic reaction networks, enabling autonomous identification of promising catalyst compositions and reaction conditions. The system relies on established machine learning techniques – specifically, graph neural networks and reinforcement learning – to iteratively refine catalyst design based on real-time feedback from simulation and, eventually, physical experimentation....

title: Discrete spatial diffusion models data while obeying scientific principles
abstract: Generative diffusion models have achieved remarkable success in producing high- quality images. However, these models typically operate in continuous intensity spaces, diffusing independently across pixels and color channels. As a result, they are fundamentally ill-suited for applications involving inherently discrete quantities—such as particle counts or material units—that are constrained by strict conservation laws like mass conservation, limiting their applicability in scientific workflows. To address this limitation, we propose Discrete Spatial Diffusion (DSD), a framework based on a continuous-time, discrete-state jump stochastic process that operates directly in discrete spatial domains while strictly preserving particle counts in both forward and reverse diffusion processes. By using spatial diffusion to achieve particle conservation, we introduce stochasticity naturally through a dis- crete formulation. We demonstrate the expressive flexibility of DSD by performing image synthesis, class conditioning, and image inpainting across standard image benchmarks, while exactly conditioning total image intensity. We validate DSD on two challenging scientific applications: porous rock microstructures and lithium- ion battery electrodes, demonstrating its ability to generate structurally realistic samples under strict mass conservation constraints, with quantitative evaluation using state-of-the-art metrics for transport and electrochemical performance.
introduction:
Introduction

Diffusion-based generative models have emerged as powerful tools for high-quality image generation [36, 66, 69]. Typically, these models inject noise into the images, then learn to reverse this noise- adding process to recover meaningful structure. In most frameworks, this is based on an Itô Stochastic Differential Equation (SDE) with Gaussian noise. While effective for many vision tasks, these approaches inherently assume continuous pixel intensities, which can cause difficulty when dealing with the discrete nature of many datasets. Tasks beyond vision, such as those in the physical sciences have many applications which require discrete physical quantities, such as particle counts in a simulation, or phases in materials microstructure. Conservation of total quantities is critical for scientific applications, and so generative modeling which can operate under constrained, discrete pixel intensities would enable scientifically grounded and physically consistent synthesis. Such a capability might also prove useful within vision tasks, such as inpainting and super-resolution.

Scientific and engineering studies of the natural world using computational techniques often involve discrete variables in space and/or time. On microscopic scales, everyday materials exhibit extremely complex structural patterns which encode the history of their formation, and play a large role in how the material functions on a macroscopic level. An important and wide-reaching field of study is materials microstructure, which is used in materials design [35], forensic analysis, hydrology [7], energy storage [65], and even medicine, such as in studies of bone structure [53]. For example,

∗Los Alamos National Laboratory

crystal grain shapes can give rise to complex stress patterns which affect the yield strength of a metal [10]. Materials microstructures are frequently represented by a small number of discrete phases that describe their underlying chemical structures. In sandstone, for example, the overall arrangement of nanocrystals is highly disordered, and gives rise to complex pore structures, through which subsurface water flows. This microstructure has an enormous influence on the rate of transport of fluids and contaminants [6]. Microstructure of electrodes is also known to have an immense impact on the characteristics of electrochemical devices [58]. Small changes in thermodynamic properties can cause drastic changes in microstructure, such as in stainless steels [79], requiring the study of microstructure as a function of phase contents. Furthermore, gathering real-world data is often complex and expensive; decades of work have been applied to computational modeling of the generation and consequences of microstructure prior to the widespread popularization of machine learning [72]. While diffusion models have been constructed for microstructure [4, 25, 37, 46, 47, 52, 80], they have not handled exact porosity (intensity) constraints.

In this work, we introduce Discrete Spatial Diffusion (DSD), a discrete-state Markov chain-based diffusion framework in which the forward process redistributes discrete units of intensity in space (Fig. 1c). Unlike previous diffusion models, DSD exactly preserves total intensity throughout both the forward and reverse phases, ensuring that global properties—such as mass fractions—are exactly conserved by representing them in terms of conserved particles. We demonstrate that DSD enables scientific applications and can extend the capabilities of conventional image processing tasks, like image generation and inpainting in discrete domains. By directly modeling discrete transitions, DSD enables generative modeling under conservation laws, allowing models that specialize for constrained conditions in scientific applications and beyond.
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline:  
Discrete Spatial Diffusion Introduces Conservation-Aware Generative Modeling for Scientific Workflows

News Article:  
A growing challenge in applying generative diffusion models to scientific and engineering domains is the need to strictly enforce conservation laws—such as mass or particle count—when generating discrete data. This limitation has recently garnered attention within the machine learning community, as highlighted by the NeurIPS publication "Fast constrained sampling in pre-trained diffusion models" [4], which explores high-quality generation under arbitrary constraints. However, most generative diffusion models in current use operate on continuous pixel intensity spaces and do not natively enforce conservation of discrete quantities essential in scientific modeling.

Addressing this fundamental gap, researchers have introduced Discrete Spatial Diffusion (DSD), a diffusion modeling framework that enforces exact conservation of discrete units throughout both the forward and reverse generative processes [Abstract][Introduction][Citation]. By operating directly in discrete spatial domains—and not simply continuous intensity spaces—DSD ensures that global properties, such as total mass or particle counts, are preserved with mathematical exactness. This is achieved by recasting the forward process as a discrete-state Markov chain, redistributing discrete intensity units across spatial locations while adhering to strict conservation constraints.

Traditional diffusion models, based on continuous Itô Stochastic Differential Equations with injected Gaussian noise, have proven highly effective in computer vision tasks but are fundamentally misaligned with the discrete, conservation-driven nature of many real-world datasets. Scientific disciplines frequently rely on models that must maintain the integrity of particle counts or phase fractions, which are central to understanding materials microstructure, hydrology, energy storage, and other fields where practical impacts are tightly coupled to microstructural configuration and composition [Introduction].

DSD’s core innovation lies in its explicit capacity to retain the total sum of discrete units—such as pixel intensities or material phases—across all generations. As a result, DSD directly models the movement and transformation of discrete particles rather than merely approximating them in a continuous domain. This not only introduces stochasticity through a discrete formulation but also delivers necessary guarantees for conservation laws, which previous diffusion models were unable to offer. Unlike prior generative approaches in this space, DSD enables synthesis strictly bounded by conservation constraints at every generative step, allowing researchers to generate valid microstructures, simulate porous rock samples, or design battery electrode configurations without violating the inherent conservation rules of their underlying systems [Abstract].

The versatility of DSD is demonstrated through its application to standard image synthesis tasks, as well as class conditioning and image inpainting, all while exactly conditioning on total image intensity. Most critically, DSD is validated on two representative and challenging scientific problems: the generation of porous rock microstructures and lithium-ion battery electrode architectures. In both domains, DSD produces structurally realistic samples under stringent mass conservation, with evaluation based on state-of-the-art metrics for transport properties and electrochemical performance [Abstract].

DSD’s discrete state-space formulation distinguishes it from earlier attempts at microstructure modeling with diffusion approaches, which failed to guarantee exact preservation of key physical quantities [Introduction]. Its contribution is therefore not only algorithmic but also principled, establishing a pathway for generative models to participate directly in science and engineering workflows that hinge on strict adherence to physical conservation.

This research positions DSD as a framework with immediate relevance to disciplines where discrete conservation is non-negotiable, and where data acquisition remains expensive or technically constrained. By advancing generative modeling under hard conservation constraints, DSD expands the reach of machine learning in scientific inquiry and design, offering a platform for physically consistent sample generation in data-scarce contexts and materials discovery.

For practitioners and researchers in computational science, DSD marks a methodological step-change toward physically meaningful generative modeling, presenting new possibilities for constrained data synthesis, simulation, and design. To explore these developments in detail, access the full paper at [https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.01917].

title: An AI approach for singleimagebased 3D character animation with preserved proportions
abstract: Reconstructing object deformation from a single image remains a significant challenge in computer vision and graphics. Existing methods typically rely on multi-view video to recover deformation, limiting their applicability under constrained scenarios. To address this, we propose DeformSplat, a novel framework that effectively guides 3D Gaussian deformation from only a single image. Our method introduces two main technical contributions. First, we present Gaussian-to-Pixel Matching which bridges the domain gap between 3D Gaussian representations and 2D pixel observations. This enables robust deformation guidance from sparse visual cues. Second, we propose Rigid Part Segmentation consisting of initialization and refinement. This segmentation explicitly identifies rigid regions, crucial for maintaining geometric coherence during deformation. By combining these two techniques, our approach can reconstruct consistent deformations from a single image. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms existing methods and naturally extends to various applications, such as frame interpolation and interactive object manipulation. Project page : https://vision3d-lab.github.io/deformsplat
introduction:
Introduction

Reconstructing object deformation from visual data is essential for creating realistic and immersive content in various media fields, such as virtual reality (VR), film, and gaming [Shuai et al. 2022]. As part of this effort, recent works have explored photorealistic rendering of deformable objects, aiming to better capture their ap- pearance and motion over time [Lu et al. 2024a; Wu et al. 2024b]. Although these methods have demonstrated strong capabilities for dynamic scene reconstruction, they often rely on multi-view or tem- porally continuous video data. Such data can be difficult to capture in real-world settings, which limits their practical applicability. This motivates the development of methods that reconstruct deformation from minimal visual inputs.

Toachievehigh-qualityandefficientreconstructionofdeformable objects, recent research has focused on scene representations that support both photorealism and editability. Among these, 3D Gauss- ian Splatting (3DGS) [Kerbl et al. 2023] has gained attention for its high-quality rendering and fast inference. Unlike implicit neural representations, such as Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) [Mildenhall

SIGGRAPH Asia 2025, December 15–18, 2025, Hong Kong, Hong Kong.

Trovato et al.

et al. 2021], 3DGS explicitly models geometry, making it both in- terpretable and easy to manipulate. Motivated by these advantages, several subsequent works have explored Gaussian-based scene edit- ing. For example, several approaches leveraging diffusion models enable intuitive editing guided by text prompts [Wu et al. 2024a] or reference images [Mei et al. 2024]. While these approaches enable intuitive edits, diffusion models often produce inconsistent results, as ambiguous text prompts lead to varied interpretations and limited geometric control. Another recent method, GESI [Luo et al. 2024], aims to address detailed geometric editing directly using a single reference image. However, it encounters difficulties in preserving intricate geometry under long-range deformations, often altering the original structure significantly. These limitations motivate our key research question: Can 3D Gaussians be deformed from a single image while preserving the original geometry?

In this work, we aim to deform a pre-reconstructed 3D Gaussian representation using only a single target image depicting a defor- mation, as illustrated in Fig. 1. Our setting is challenging, as we aim to deform 3D Gaussians using only a single RGB image, unlike conventional methods that rely on richer inputs such as multiple views or video sequences. The absence of depth and camera pose information further complicates the deformation process in our case. This constrained setting gives rise to two key challenges. The first challenge is determining how and in which direction the Gaussians should deform when only a single viewpoint is available. This is difficult because a single image provides only partial observations of the 3D structure, making it hard to infer meaningful deformation cues. To extract meaningful deformation cues under this constraint, it is essential to establish reliable correspondences between the 2D image and the 3D Gaussians. The second challenge is to prevent overfitting to the single input image, which can result in unwanted geometric distortions due to the lack of depth or multi-view con- straints. Thus, preserving original geometry is crucial, especially in rigid regions that should remain unchanged during deformation.

To address these challenges, we propose a novel framework called DeformSplat, consisting of two main components: Gaussian-to- Pixel Matching and Rigid Part Segmentation. Gaussian-to-Pixel Matching aims to guide the deformation by linking visually similar regions between the 3D Gaussians and the target image. Specifically, we render multi-view images from the 3D Gaussians and compute pixel-wise correspondences between each rendered image and the target image using an image matcher. Based on the pixel-to-pixel matching, we select the viewpoint with the largest visual overlap, and translate its correspondences into Gaussian-to-Pixel mappings. This step provides an essential basis for deformation, enabling the Gaussians to reflect the geometry depicted in the target image.

To further ensure geometric consistency, we propose Rigid Part Segmentation that explicitly identifies rigid regions within the Gaussian representation. To achieve this, our method first initial- izes rigid groups based on Gaussian-to-Pixel correspondences and then iteratively refines these groups during optimization. The seg- mentation is used in a rigidity-aware optimization that regularizes rigid and non-rigid regions differently to preserve geometry. Conse- quently, we achieve superior performance than previous SOTA and generalize to applications such as frame interpolation and interac- tive object manipulation.

SIGGRAPH Asia 2025, December 15–18, 2025, Hong Kong, Hong Kong.

Our contributions can be summarized as follows:

We propose DeformSplat, a novel framework for rigidity- aware deformation of 3D Gaussians using only a single target image.

We present Gaussian-to-Pixel Matching strategy that con- nects the 3D Gaussian representation with the 2D target image to guide deformation.

WeproposeRigidPartSegmentationmethod,whichpreserves the original geometry by detecting rigid regions and con- straining their deformation.

Our method shows superior performance in single image Gaussian deformation and extends to applications such as frame interpolation and interactive manipulation.

Headline:  
DeformSplat Unveils Rigidity-Aware 3D Gaussian Deformation from a Single Image, Advancing Interactive Media Applications

News Article:  
Recent advances in single-image 3D reconstruction are redefining what is possible in interactive media creation and virtual content generation. While earlier mesh-based frameworks—such as those achieving high-precision 3D reconstructions of objects like eyeglasses from a single RGB image—demonstrated technical rigor, their reliance on mesh representations and object-specific adaptation limited broader applicability [Anchor Event, Elsevier, 2026]. Addressing these constraints, DeformSplat introduces a new paradigm: reconstructing and deforming 3D Gaussian representations of general objects using only a single image (Kerbl et al., 2025; source: https://vision3d-lab.github.io/deformsplat).

The core innovation of DeformSplat lies in its departure from multi-view or temporally continuous data requirements, which have historically restricted photorealistic deformation methods to controlled acquisition settings [Abstract, Introduction]. Instead, DeformSplat is structured around two technical contributions that together close longstanding gaps in the single-image scenario: Gaussian-to-Pixel Matching and Rigid Part Segmentation.

Gaussian-to-Pixel Matching is a key mechanism enabling this advance. The framework establishes correspondences between the visual cues present in a single 2D target image and the underlying 3D Gaussian representation—rendered from multiple virtual perspectives. By identifying the rendered view with maximal overlap to the target image and extracting pixel-level matches, DeformSplat generates actionable guidance for geometric deformation from highly sparse visual input [Abstract, Introduction]. This strategy circumvents the lack of multi-view or depth information, a persistent bottleneck in previous methods, enabling deformation rooted in robust visual correspondences rather than relying predominantly on ambiguous or unconstrained generative models. The method’s impact is immediately evident: it operationalizes precise object deformation from cases where only a single perspective is available, thus opening up new possibilities for practical deployment in situations with limited data capture.

Complementing this, Rigid Part Segmentation addresses another persistent deficit in single-image deformation—the need to preserve intrinsic geometric stability, particularly in regions of the object meant to remain structurally consistent. DeformSplat’s segmentation process identifies and isolates these rigid regions within the 3D Gaussian space, using an iterative optimization to refine the segmentation throughout training. The resulting rigidity-aware approach ensures that deformation effects remain localized and that essential geometries are maintained, mitigating overfitting and distortion risks that frequently plague prior single-image techniques [Abstract, Introduction]. This explicit protection of rigid structure underpins DeformSplat’s strength in reconstructing deformations that look plausible and retain fidelity to the original shape, especially where arbitrary deformations or inconsistent structural edits previously occurred.

The practical implications for content creators and computer graphics practitioners are significant. By eliminating the dependence on multi-view sequences or depth annotations, DeformSplat enables 3D geometry manipulation and animation from minimal visual cues. Applications that previously required labor-intensive data acquisition—such as frame interpolation, interactive object manipulation, and rapid prototyping for VR or AR content—can now leverage this streamlined approach, thereby accelerating both creative and technical workflows. In contrast to mesh-based systems narrowly tailored to specific object types, DeformSplat’s Gaussian formulation offers generality across a wide class of objects and scenarios, broadening the usable frontier for single-image 3D deformation.

By integrating Gaussian-to-Pixel Matching and Rigid Part Segmentation, DeformSplat establishes a new standard for single-image-based geometric editing, outperforming contemporary state-of-the-art models that struggle to maintain structural coherence under deformation or generalize beyond narrowly defined problem settings [Abstract, Introduction, Citation]. The methodology’s explicit handling of geometric constraints, alongside reliable extraction of deformation signals from sparse input, defines its marked methodological progress and practical utility.

As workflows in interactive media, computer graphics, and virtual content creation increasingly demand flexibility and high fidelity from minimal data, DeformSplat’s unified Gaussian-based framework signals a pivotal evolution. For domain experts seeking to leverage or extend these innovations, further technical details and experimental validations are available in the published work and project page ([SIGGRAPH Asia 2025; https://vision3d-lab.github.io/deformsplat](https://vision3d-lab.github.io/deformsplat)). Readers are strongly encouraged to engage directly with the source material to assess potential applications and inspire new research directions anchored in this framework.
