Headline:  
Language Models as Architects of Their Own Inference: DISCIPL and the New Era of Self-Steering Probabilistic Programs

News Article:  
The automation of probabilistic inference has undergone significant evolution, marked most notably by the adoption of probabilistic programming languages (PPLs) within both database engineering and machine learning research. Benchmarking frameworks clarify how PPLs relieve developers from the arduous requirement to custom-infer models, relying instead on standardized, executable probabilistic procedures (see: "Benchmark of Probabilistic Programming Languages"). This automated approach contextualizes the technical advancements debuted in DISCIPL, a new method for language model (LM) self-steering at inference time, which defines a novel intersection between probabilistic programming and contemporary LM reasoning.

A perennial challenge in LM research is the gap between a model’s abstract reasoning proficiency and its reliability at constrained, complex tasks. While state-of-the-art LMs excel on scientific and mathematical benchmarks, their performance on domains such as rule-based text generation, constrained numeric reasoning, and combinatorial puzzle-solving remains inconsistent, even when significant computational resources are allocated. Existing inference-time methods branch into two dominant paradigms: serialized chain-of-thought-style reasoning or externalized structured inference (tree search, sequential Monte Carlo methods). The former, while flexibly managed via autoregressive generation, incurs substantial computation overhead and unstable output fidelity. The latter improves parallelism and efficiency but typically depends on domain-specific hand engineering and requires pre-defined verifiers or scoring metrics, restricting generality and scaling.

DISCIPL (Distributional Constraints by Inference Programming with Language Models) introduces a meta-reasoning framework designed to allow LMs to program and guide their own inference procedures in real time. In this architecture, a Planner LM synthesizes a bespoke inference program that formalizes both the problem specification—encoding requirements and constraints—and the stepwise stochastic procedure that attempts to solve the task. This program orchestrates a population of Follower LMs, each instantiating the collective, parallelized search for solutions that satisfy the Planner’s constraints (Abstract; Introduction).

The technical crux is the bifurcation of the LM’s role into planning and execution modules. The Planner, which may be instantiated by a high-capability model such as GPT-4o, generates explicit inference code–in practice, lightweight probabilistic programs–that smaller Follower LMs (e.g., Llama-3.2-1B or Qwen3-1.7B) then execute. Follower LMs are tasked with generating candidate outputs according to the Planner’s logic, and the overall system dynamically allocates computational resources to balance efficiency and constraint satisfaction. This design enables highly parallelized Monte Carlo inference strategies, which are directly compatible with advances in LM architectures and require neither further finetuning nor manual scorer design.

Empirical evaluation of DISCIPL leverages two primary domains. First, the COLLIE benchmark provides a suite of constrained generation tasks that consistently challenge even the most advanced LMs. Second, a custom PUZZLES dataset spans specialized tasks in poetry, grant-writing, budgeting, and itinerary planning, each with tightly-defined formal and stylistic requirements. In these settings, results demonstrate that DISCIPL enables low-parameter Follower LMs to match or surpass the performance of substantially larger models, including GPT-4o and o1, on tasks where standard inference methods falter. Notably, DISCIPL achieves this while decoupling reasoning ability from parameter count–boosting accuracy on paragraph-level generation and enabling stringent constraint satisfaction on complex puzzle formulations.

The architecture’s general applicability is highlighted by its automated compositional design: LMs themselves generate the very inference procedures they later execute. This generative-inferential interplay is foundationally distinct from fixed-model or purely externally-defined Monte Carlo approaches. It expands the design space for inference-driven LM research, holding technical continuity with the automation legacy of PPLs but removing the requirement for human-designed verifiers, thereby facilitating test-time scaling in previously inaccessible problem spaces (Abstract; Introduction; Citation).

For the research community, DISCIPL’s implications extend beyond benchmarking. It directly invokes the computational ethos pioneered by PPLs, wherein verifiable, executable inference is generated, managed, and evaluated automatically without the manual intervention typically demanded by prior art. This paradigm provides a robust path forward for leveraging rapid advancements in LM architecture, while retaining modularity in reasoning and inference design at runtime.

To examine the technical details, evaluate code samples, or reproduce benchmarking results, readers are motivated to directly consult the full paper, "DISCIPL: Distributional Constraints by Inference Programming with Language Models," available at https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2504.07081.

This benchmarking work offers a comparative technical perspective crucial to situating DISCIPL’s innovations within the broader evolution of automated inference in probabilistic programming.

title: Resolving to spend less time on your smartphone Understanding your travel habits can help, say researchers
abstract: Habits are a fundamental driver of human behavior. However, prior research has largely studied habits in isolation. This study investigates spatial and smartphone habits—two cornerstone habits in contemporary society—in tandem, revealing the multiplicative ways in which habitual processes can underlie human behavior. We provide evidence that smartphone habits are stronger in habitually traveled and visited spaces. We leverage a custom-designed app that logged mobility and app use while prompting map-based questionnaires of participants’ trips for two weeks (N=419 participants; n=27,446,977 GPS and app logs; n=7,226 trip questionnaires). Overall, app habit strength predicted higher likelihood of app use (vs. non-use) and more app use (when used) on more habitually travelled routes and in more habitually visited destinations. The interaction between spatial and smartphone habits was most consistent for objective (vs. subjective) habit indicators. Social app habits were stronger and more context-independent than non-social app habits. Altogether, our findings reveal how distinct habitual processes can combine to shape daily behavior. Further, our method illustrates the potential of linking mobile sensing and surveying to measure habits in the real world.
introduction:
Habits are a direct and deep-rooted driver of human behavior1,2. By automating our cognition, habitual processes can make the world easier to navigate. Yet, they can also conflict with our goals3,4. For better and for worse, the degree to which behavior is performed habitually has powerful implications for how we engage with the world around us. Although people engage in a wide variety of habits in daily life, habits are typically studied in isolation5. By zooming out from this standard scope, we can reveal broader, multiplicative ways that habitual processes underlie real-world behavior across domains of everyday life.

Habitual behaviors are cued and performed automatically after being frequently rewarded in stable contexts2. In turn, the strength of a habit is indicated by the level of frequency (i.e., number of occurrences) and automaticity (i.e., lack of conscious processes)6. In contemporary society, spatial and smartphone habits act as cornerstone habits. Although a variety of factors can contribute to mobility choices and mobile phone use in a given situation, past research across disciplines indicates that habit is a leading predictor of both7,8. Critically, the interaction between these two types of habits is ideally suited for analyzing interactive habitual processes in daily life, as smartphone habits are uniquely activated across spaces9,10. Exploring their interactive relationship is also vital given the growing integration of mobility and technology: a convergence with implications for numerous facets of human behavior11–16.

Spatial habits are a prominent form of habitual behavior, as people often select and engage with their everyday spaces out of habit7,8. Scholars have extensively examined the role of habit in the selection of routes17–21, or how individuals travel from an origin to a destination via a particular mode of transportation (e.g., walking). Here, we focus on the role of habit in how people select routes as well as destinations, which together comprise the activity spaces that people inhabit in daily life22. Thus, spatial habits are comprised of places (i.e., destinations) and how individuals move between them (i.e., routes). Spatial habits can be cued by myriad factors (e.g., time, trip purpose)23. Research in cognitive geography has investigated spatial habits through the study of navigation, spatial cognition, and mental mapping. A notable example is anchor-point theory, which examines how people perceive and represent space through reference points like landmarks or neighborhoods24. Moreover, the centrality of spatial behavior in past habit research reflects the repetitive nature of daily mobility patterns identified in large-scale computational studies25,26. Spatial habits thereby have unique potential to operate as foundational habits: they determine the stable contexts that offer cues (or 'triggers') for other daily habits.

Smartphone habits are another dominant form of habitual behavior in modern society, as well as a heuristic case in habit research27. By bundling many possible behaviors (e.g., social media scrolling, podcast listening) into one handheld object, smartphones facilitate the development of a wide variety of activation cues (e.g., times, user activities), including personalized sets of cues tied to specific apps28. Accordingly, smartphone habits can be uniquely activated across a variety of spatial contexts9,10—challenging the presumed 'context-dependence' of traditional media habits and presumed role of location for cueing habits at-large2. However, some studies reveal that certain types of apps are also more likely to be used in certain types of places, as routes and destinations afford certain activities29,30. In this way, specific smartphone behaviors may develop implicit associations with specific types of spaces31,32—such as more habitually selected contexts33. In other words, spatial habits have the potential to make smartphone habits stronger.

To test this proposition, we examine whether more habitual apps are used disproportionately more in (1) habitually travelled routes and (2) habitually visited destinations. We test whether habitual apps can be interwoven with transportation habits by evaluating app use on routes. For example, app use and specific spaces may become embedded in scripts34: a habitual route (e.g., driving from home to work) can cue an app habit (e.g., listening to Spotify) at rehearsed points during the trip (e.g., traffic lights). These scripts may also be prompted by specific cues (e.g., time of day, social setting), thereby coupling spatial habits (e.g., the morning commute) and smartphone habits (e.g., listening to a news podcast) through sequences of cued actions. Additionally, we test the downstream influence of spatial habits on smartphone use by evaluating app use in destinations (e.g., the workplace). Since spaces chosen out of habit are more familiar35, habitually visited destinations may be more likely to contain familiar cues that activate app habits (e.g., checking a social media app while cleaning the kitchen). Altogether, we test whether app habits are activated more when individuals choose routes and destinations out of habit.

We further examine the robustness of the interaction between spatial and smartphone habits by exploring the distinctive role of social vs. non-social apps. Social apps provide a direct channel for social needs and norms to be activated habitually in daily life36. Based on the potency of social rewards in habit formation, along with the high proportion of social app usage in studies of overall smartphone usage37,38, social apps are expected to exhibit higher habit strength27,39. The theorized strength of social app habits may derive from their reliance on internal, non-spatial cues40. For example, people may habitually check social apps throughout the day in response to or in anticipation of new messages27,37. Thus, social app habits may be more likely to be context-independent: triggered across a variety of spatial contexts39.

Habits present a unique measurement challenge because they are triggered and performed with minimal conscious awareness throughout daily life41. Traditionally, habit research has centered on establishing the causal role of habit versus more conscious processes such as goals27. Here, moving beyond the question of whether habits or goals matter more, we focus on the real-world conditions—or contexts—under which habits become more likely to be activated. Accordingly, the current study sought to capture habits in the real world. By coupling GPS and app logs with map-based questionnaires, we collected objective and subjective indicators of spatial and smartphone habits and used those indicators to predict app use in context.

Headline: How Commuting Routines Shape Smartphone Habits: New Research Illuminates the Interplay of Movement and Mobile Use

News Article:

If you find yourself reaching for your smartphone while boarding the bus or following your usual route to work, you are not alone. Emerging research underscores a tightly linked relationship between commuting and smartphone behavior, revealing that travel habits can reinforce phone habits—a dynamic with significant consequences for daily life. As highlighted in recent coverage [Anchor Event], understanding this connection is critical not only for personal well-being but also for aligning technology use with larger life goals.

In a recent study published in Scientific Reports ([Citation]), researchers provide new insights into how habitual actions in physical space—referred to as spatial habits—interact with habits formed around smartphone app use. The authors demonstrate that these cornerstones of modern behavior do not operate in isolation; rather, habitual movement through the world strengthens the likelihood and intensity of smartphone use, especially with apps people already use out of habit. This multiplicative relationship situates habitual processes at the foundation of daily routines, complicating efforts to alter technology usage without considering one’s broader behavioral context.

The main technical finding is clear: the more individuals follow familiar routes or visit the same destinations—a demonstration of spatial habits—the stronger their smartphone habits become in those contexts. In other words, people are more likely to use their habitual apps, and to use them more, when traveling on well-trodden paths or visiting frequently chosen locations. This effect is observed most robustly with objective measures of behavior, such as logging actual GPS and app use data, rather than relying solely on self-report.

To uncover these patterns, the research team designed a custom mobile application that logged the mobility patterns and app usage of 419 participants over two weeks, amassing over 27 million GPS and app logs. Participants also responded to map-based questionnaires about their daily trips. By analyzing this real-world data, the study was able to systematically measure both spatial and smartphone habit strength, as well as how they interact in practice ([Abstract], [Introduction], [Citation]).

The implications extend beyond simple observation. This work demonstrates that spatial habits not only serve as triggers—or cues—for digital habits but also create stable behavioral contexts in which smartphone app use flourishes. For example, someone who regularly commutes the same way might also regularly use a specific app along that route, embedding both activities into a larger behavioral “script.”

Moreover, the study finds that the relationship between spatial habits and smartphone behavior varies by app type. Habits involving social apps—such as messaging or social media platforms—are not only stronger but also less reliant on specific physical locations. People tend to check social apps across a wide range of environments, likely due to the internal, non-spatial cues that prompt these interactions. Meanwhile, use of other app types is more likely to be intertwined with specific places or routines.

For individuals seeking to modify their smartphone use, these findings highlight the value of examining their movement patterns. Changing where and how one travels may provide opportunities to disrupt automatic phone checking or app use. For instance, altering a daily commute, even in small ways, could help weaken the spatial cues that reinforce digital habits—a concrete strategy for those aiming to reduce screen time.

On an organizational level, these results suggest that interventions designed to address technology overuse should not focus only on the digital domain. Instead, considering the physical movement patterns of users and the environments in which app use is triggered may reveal new leverage points for supporting intentional technology engagement. For example, settings that encourage varied movement or conscious route selection might help reduce automatic phone use, especially in workplace or educational environments.

Ultimately, the study’s methodological contribution is as notable as its behavioral insights. By combining objective mobile sensing with context-sensitive surveys, the researchers offer a new approach for assessing the strength and interaction of habits as they unfold in daily life, outside laboratory settings. This integrated methodology opens pathways for future work aimed at understanding and reshaping habitual behavior in real-world contexts.

For those interested in the intersection of technology, behavior, and everyday context, this research underscores the power and complexity of habit formation. To meaningfully alter digital habits, it may be necessary to rethink the spaces and routines that envelop our daily lives.

Access the full study for a deeper exploration of these findings and the methodology underpinning them: https://dx.doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-25174-2

title: Researchers reveal bias in a widely used measure of algorithm performance
abstract:
Normalized mutual information is a biased measure for classification and community detection

Normalized mutual information is widely used as a similarity measure for evaluating the In this paper, we argue that results performance of clustering and classification algorithms. returned by the normalized mutual information are biased for two reasons: first, because they ignore the information content of the contingency table and, second, because their symmetric normalization introduces spurious dependence on algorithm output. We introduce a modified version of the mutual information that remedies both of these shortcomings. As a practical demonstration of the importance of using an unbiased measure, we perform extensive numer- ical tests on a basket of popular algorithms for network community detection and show that one’s conclusions about which algorithm is best are significantly affected by the biases in the traditional mutual information.
introduction:
1 Introduction

A common task in data analysis is the comparison of two different labelings of a set of objects. How well do demographics predict political affiliation? How accurately do blood tests predict clinical outcomes? These are questions about how similar one labeling of individuals/items/results is to another and they are commonly tackled using the information theoretic measure known as mutual information, in which an experimental or computational measure of some kind is compared against a ground truth.

Mutual information works by asking of how efficiently we can describe one labeling if we already know the other [1]. Specifically, it measures how much less information it takes to communicate the first labeling if we know the second versus if we do not. As an example, mutual information is commonly used in network science to evaluate the performance of algorithms for network commu- nity detection [2]. One takes a network whose community structure is already known and applies a community detection algorithm to it to infer the communities. Then one uses mutual information to compare the output of the algorithm to the known correct communities. Algorithms that con-

sistently achieve high mutual information scores are considered good. We will use this application as an illustrative example later in the paper.

Mutual information has a number of appealing properties as a tool for comparing labelings. It is invariant under permutations of the labels, so that, for example, two divisions of a network into communities that differ only in the numbering of the communities will be correctly identified as being the same. It also returns sensible results in cases where the number of distinct label values is not the same in the two labelings. However, the standard mutual information measure also has some significant shortcomings, and two in particular that we highlight in this paper. First, it has a bias towards labelings with too many distinct label values. For instance, a community detection algorithm that incorrectly divides networks into significantly more groups than are present in the ground truth can nonetheless achieve a high mutual information score. A number of approaches for correcting this flaw have been proposed. One can apply direct penalties for incorrect numbers of groups [3] or subtract correction terms based on the average mutual information over some ensemble of candidate labelings [4, 5] or on the statistics of the contingency table [6, 7]. For reasons we discuss shortly, we favor the latter approach, which leads to the measure known as the reduced mutual information.

The second drawback of the mutual information arises when the measure is normalized, as it commonly is to improve interpretability. The most popular normalization scheme creates a measure that runs between zero and one by dividing the mutual information by the arithmetic mean of the entropies of the two labelings being compared [8], although one can also normalize by the minimum, maximum, or geometric mean of the entropies. As we demonstrate in this paper, however, these normalizations introduce biases into the results by comparison with the unnormalized measure, because the normalization factor depends on the candidate labeling as well as the ground truth. This effect can be large enough to change scientific outcomes, and we provide examples of this phenomenon.

In order to avoid this latter bias, while still retaining the interpretability of a normalized mutual information measure, we favor normalizing by the entropy of the ground-truth labeling alone. This removes the source of bias but introduces an asymmetry in the normalization. At first sight this asymmetry may seem undesirable, and previous authors have gone to some lengths to avoid it. Here, however, we argue that it is not only justified but actually desirable, for several reasons. First, many of the classification problems we consider are unaffected by the asymmetry, since they involve the comparison of one or more candidate labelings against a single, unchanging ground truth. Moreover, by contrast with the multitude of possible symmetric normalizations, the asymmetric measure we propose is the unique way to normalize the mutual information without introducing biases due to the normalization itself.

More broadly, the asymmetric measure is more informative than the conventional symmetric one. Consider for instance the common situation where the groups or communities in one labeling are a subdivision of those in the other. We might for example label individuals by the country they live in on the one hand and by the town or city on the other. Then the more detailed labeling tells us everything there is to know about the coarser one, but the reverse is not true. Telling you the city fixes the country, but not vice versa. Thus one could argue that the mutual information between the two eshould be asymmetric, and this type of asymmetry will be a key feature of the measures we study.

Both drawbacks of the standard mutual information—bias towards too many groups and depen- dence of the normalization on the candidate labeling—can be addressed simultaneously by using an asymmetric normalized reduced mutual information as defined in this paper. In support of this approach we present an extensive comparison of the performance of this and other variants of the mutual information in network community detection tasks, generating a large number of random

test networks with known community structure and a variety of structural parameters, and then attempting to recover the communities using popular community detection algorithms. Within this framework, we find that conclusions about which algorithms perform best are significantly impacted by the choice of mutual information measure, and specifically that traditional measures erroneously favor algorithms that find too many communities, but our proposed measure does not. Code im- plementing our measure can be found at https://github.com/maxjerdee/clustering-mi.
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline:  
Researchers Identify Critical Bias in Widely Used Algorithm Evaluation Metric

News Article:  
In a study published in _Nature Communications_ ([DOI: 10.1038/s41467-025-66150-8](https://dx.doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-66150-8)), Max Jerdee (Santa Fe Institute), Alec Kirkley (University of Hong Kong), and Mark Newman (University of Michigan) demonstrate that normalized mutual information—the standard metric for evaluating clustering and classification algorithms—introduces substantive bias, fundamentally altering algorithmic assessment outcomes. The findings, summarized by the Santa Fe Institute, reveal that biases in normalized mutual information can be strong enough to overturn scientific conclusions about algorithm performance, with the metric favoring results that are mathematically misleading.

Normalized mutual information (NMI) is often applied to measure the similarity between computational groupings of data and a ground truth. In thousands of research applications, NMI is used as a benchmark to decide which model or algorithm most accurately detects clusters or communities within complex datasets. According to Newman, “it turns out that it can give incorrect results, and the errors are large enough to change scientific conclusions in some cases.”

The technical core of the issue lies in two main sources of bias. First, NMI can reward algorithms that divide datasets into too many groups—even if those divisions are inaccurate. This creates a perverse incentive for algorithms, making over-segmentation seem more successful than it actually is. Second, the commonly used normalization process in NMI can introduce additional bias, particularly when comparing algorithm outputs to established ground truth data. This further distorts assessments, especially when candidate results vary widely from the true grouping.

The authors argue that these biases are not minor, but structurally significant: using NMI in its standard form can lead to mischaracterizing which algorithms are superior—a critical problem in real-world research settings such as network science, social systems modeling, and biomedical classification. Their empirical tests on community detection algorithms confirmed that traditional NMI not only fails to penalize over-partitioning, but also misguides researchers in comparative evaluations.

To address these issues, Jerdee, Kirkley, and Newman introduce an alternative metric: asymmetric normalized reduced mutual information. This revised measure removes the dependence on the algorithm’s output and instead calibrates the comparison using only the ground-truth structure of the data. The resulting metric is less susceptible to distortions from over-segmentation or spurious normalization effects. The researchers maintain that this approach is not just theoretically robust, but more practically appropriate for the common scenario of comparing candidate classifications to a single, fixed ground truth.

The broader significance of this work is clear: reliance on standard NMI can distort algorithm performance comparisons and potentially lead researchers to erroneous conclusions. By adopting the asymmetric normalized reduced mutual information, researchers can avoid these pitfalls and attain more reliable and interpretable evaluation metrics.

Researchers or practitioners working in clustering, classification, or community detection are encouraged to consult the full paper and consider adopting this improved metric in their analyses. The code implementing the proposed measure is available at [https://github.com/maxjerdee/clustering-mi](https://github.com/maxjerdee/clustering-mi). For in-depth discussion and empirical benchmarks, access the research at _Nature Communications_ ([DOI: 10.1038/s41467-025-66150-8](https://dx.doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-66150-8)).

anchor_event.full_content:
**Read the paper “Normalized mutual information is a biased measure for classification and community detection” in _Nature Communications_ (December 11, 2025). DOI: [10.1038/s41467-025-66150-8](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-66150-8)**

In a paper published in Nature Communications, SFI Postdoctoral Fellow [Max Jerdee](https://www.santafe.edu/people/profile/max-jerdee), Alec Kirkley (University of Hong Kong), and SFI External Professor [Mark Newman](https://www.santafe.edu/people/profile/mark-newman) (University of Michigan) show that NMI — one of the most widely used metrics in data science and network research — can produce biased results. "Normalized mutual information has been used or referenced in thousands of papers in the decades since it was first proposed,” Newman says, “but it turns out that it can give incorrect results, and the errors are large enough to change scientific conclusions in some cases."

First, it can reward algorithms that over-divide data, inventing extra categories and appearing more accurate than they are. Second, commonly used normalization methods can introduce a further bias toward artificially simple algorithms. Both effects can distort comparisons, especially in complex problems where the “true” grouping is not straightforward.

title: AI can pick up cultural values by mimicking how kids learn
abstract:
Constructing a universal moral code for artificial intelligence (AI) is challenging because human cultures have different values, norms, and social practices. We

therefore argue that AI systems should adapt to culture based on observation: Just

as a child raised in a particular culture learns the specific values, norms, and behav-
introduction:
1 Introduction

A formidable challenge in the field of artificial intelligence (AI) is endowing AI agents with the richness and depth of values and behaviors that characterize human cultures and their decision-making tendencies. State-of-the-art AI systems today [1–3] are based on large-language models (LLMs), which are neural networks with billions of parameters trained on data harvested from across the internet, leading

Data availability statement: Code and data for replicating the findings in this article are available at: https:// github.com/JasmineLi-805/moral-ai-irl.

to a “one-size-fits-all” (rather than pluralistic) AI system [4,5] (see also [6,7]). In this article, we propose that rather than attempting to handcraft or learn a universal code of values and behaviors for AI, AI agents should implicitly learn them by being embedded in the human culture in which they are operating [8]. We use the term “culture” here to refer to the dynamic values, norms, and behaviors shared by a social group [9], acknowledging that it is a dynamic phenomenon—a cultural cycle in which humans continuously shape and adopt values and behaviors [10,11]. The approach we propose would endow AI agents with the ability to deal with such cul- tural dynamics in a manner that is tailored to the culture or social group in which the AI agent was “raised.” This recognizes the fact that what is acceptable or norma- tive in one culture may not be acceptable in another [12], necessitating AI systems that are capable of adapting to the values and behavioral tendencies of the culture in which they have been deployed. This will be challenging for AI and some intellec- tual humility is demanded, in as much as even humans, the creators of the AI, have a hard time appreciating and accepting the values and behaviors of cultures different from their own [12,13]. Might future AI surpass humans in such flexibility?

Funding: This work was supported by the Templeton World Charity Foundation (RPNR, ANM), the National Science Foundation EFRI Grant no. 2223495 (RPNR) and Grant no. 2230466 (KR, ANM), a UW + Amazon Science Hub grant (RPNR), a grant from the Bezos Family Foundation (ANM), and the Cherng Jia & Elizabeth Yun Hwang Professorship (RPNR). https://www.templetonworldcharity.org/ https://www.nsf.gov/ https://www.sciencehub.uw.edu/ https://www.bezosfamilyfoundation.org/ The funders did not play any role in the study design, data collection and analysis, decision to publish, or preparation of the manuscript.

Competing interests: The authors have declared that no competing interests exist.

1.1 Altruism as a cultural value for AI to learn

In this article, we focus on the human tendency to act in a way that benefits others, even if it comes at a cost to oneself, often labeled “altruistic behavior.” For example, a person might give precious resources (such as food, money, or time) to someone else without directly benefiting themselves. In cases of extreme altruism, these acts can be directed towards strangers even in situations when the actor has no expectancy for reciprocal action or reward. There are significant between-country differences in altruistic tendencies across industrialized nations [14], small-scale traditional cultures [15], and even in children as a function of cultural background [16], motivating the need to investigate whether an AI system could learn altruistic behaviors from observation.

Our approach draws on evidence about how neural mechanisms in the brain support normative social behavior: the same reward systems thought to be involved in reinforcement-based learning are also harnessed to learn the dominant norms and values within a society [17–19]. The resulting system governs human cultural expectations and ways of interacting with others [20,21]. To learn what the accepted behavior is in any given context, the brain must first develop expectation rules about events and learn internal “value systems,” including the benefits of certain behav- iors as well as the costs of deviating from the norm. For example, by perceiving or producing altruistic behaviors within a culture—such as giving others items of value or otherwise acting to benefit others even at a cost to oneself—a child or an ado- lescent may learn to connect, in a certain context, a “positive” value with behaving altruistically [16,22].

There has been considerable AI research on using inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) and imitation learning to mimic human actions [23–29]. More recently, IRL has been used to learn human value systems in specific contexts such as route choice modeling [30], and multi-objective IRL/RL methods have been proposed for

human value alignment [31–34]. Here, we use IRL to address the question: Can an AI agent learn cultural values and behavioral tendencies for specific social scenarios by observing the behavior of humans from different cultural groups, and then generalize this learning to a new context?

1.2 Using inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) to train AI on human culture

IRL is a technique developed in the field of machine learning [23,26,28] to enable an AI agent to learn a new skill from human demonstrations; rather than simply mimicking the exact actions of the human, IRL attempts to learn a “reward function” from the human demonstrations. The reward function assigns positive or negative values (“rewards” or “penal- ties”) to particular features of the observed demonstration. By learning an appropriate reward function that is aligned with the presumed reward function being used by the human in the demonstrations, the AI agent is able to imitate the human behavior and generalize beyond simple mimicry of human actions.

In this article, we show how IRL can be used to train AI agents that can infer and learn the reward functions of humans belonging to a particular cultural group. The learned reward function assigns specific reward values (positive or negative) to situations in which the AI agent may find itself. By choosing actions that optimize total expected reward based on its learned reward function, the AI agent can behave in a manner that is attuned to the human culture in which it is embed- ded. A major advantage of this approach is that the agent can assign reward values to scenarios that are related to but not exactly the same as previously encountered scenarios. This allows the agent to generalize its decision-making ability, including those involving societal values and behavioral tendencies, to novel situations, emulating the first- and second- order generalization children demonstrate based on cultural learning and experience [16,35,36].

To test this approach, we collected data from human participants playing an online game involving simple forms of altruism and fairness, both of which have been regarded as important components of human morality and culture [37–42]. We recruited participants with cultural backgrounds associated with different altruistic behaviors [43], specifically, US participants who identified as White or as Latino (we use the term “Latino” to denote anyone born in or with ancestors from Latin America, regardless of gender).

Our hypothesis regarding differences in altruistic behavior is motivated by prior work showing that, within the same nation-state (here, the US), Latinos tend to be more collectivistic [44,45] and tend to have a more interdependent self- concept than Whites, who are generally characterized as being more individualistic and prone to seeing themselves as independent of others [46–48]. Whereas individualistic cultures tend to emphasize personal achievement and autonomy, collectivist cultures often prioritize group harmony and the achievements and welfare of others over personal gains. We hypothesize that, within the novel context of our online “world,” Latinos will exhibit more altruistic behavior than Whites. That is, subgroups of people within the US share cultural tendencies that they may bring to an online game, which we hypothesize can be inferred through IRL by an AI agent.

Note that rather than using data from human adults who make explicit value judgments, as in the case of the Moral Machines experiment involving the ethics of self-driving cars [49] or the trolley problem [50,51] (see also [52]), or data from descriptive judgments of scenarios presenting values and norms, as in the Delphi experiment [53] (see also [4,5]), our approach seeks to emulate the way a human child absorbs values and norms implicitly from observation and inter- actions with people in their culture [16,54]. Our approach shares similarities with previous work in reinforcement learn- ing [55,56] and brain-inspired affective learning [57] showing how an AI agent could learn ethical characteristics such as altruism, empathy, and sympathy; these approaches did not train their AI agent on human data (as in our study here) and did not focus on cultural differences. Finally, our online game scenario includes an element of fairness, in addition to altruism: the “privileged” player has to endure some costs to help the other player who has been placed in an unfair and potentially less rewarding circumstance. We hypothesize that the reward functions inferred by IRL from human data may implicitly capture this aspect of the game scenario.

To summarize, this article tests the following hypotheses:

H1: Cultural background and exposure to altruistic behavior may influence participants’ tendencies to display altruistic

behavior.

H2: An AI agent using inverse reinforcement learning can learn culturally attuned, quantifiable altruistic behaviors and

values for specific social scenarios by observing the behavior of humans from different cultural groups.

H3: An AI agent can generalize learned social values and behavior to a new context.

The remainder of the article focuses on the above hypothesis and presents our results. We first describe our experimen- tal studies with humans and present results showing that an AI agent can learn from observing the behavior of a particular cultural group (Latinos or Whites) an IRL reward function capturing the altruistic tendencies of that group. We then show how this learning can generalize to new problems involving other altruistic decision making. Taken together, these results, though obtained from a simple online game scenario, point the way towards future AI agents that could become attuned to the culture in which they are operating by continually learning and adjusting their behavior from observing and interacting with humans within that culture.

Headline: Towards Culturally Adaptive AI: New Research Demonstrates Altruistic Value Learning Across Human Groups

News Article:

A recent peer initiative from San Diego State University has spotlighted the pressing challenge of how artificial intelligence (AI) systems can mirror the nuanced fabric of human decision-making across diverse cultural contexts. The study explored whether AI, by merely observing human decision-making, can learn and generalize culturally specific tendencies—such as altruism—when presented with new scenarios. This fundamental question has spurred broader technical investigations into how AI can move beyond "one-size-fits-all" architectures and achieve deeper cultural attunement [Real-World Anchor Event].

The dominant paradigm in current AI, primarily driven by large language models (LLMs) and reinforcement learning, results in systems whose behavioral outputs often default to global averages due to the internet-spanning training corpora. Such an approach presupposes static and universal value sets, undercutting the ability of these agents to exhibit the heterogeneity fundamental to human cultures [Introduction]. The technical core of this limitation lies in the reward functions underlying RL-based AI: these functions are typically hand-crafted or learned from datasets in a way that blurs or ignores the distinctions between cultural values and behavioral expectations [Abstract][Introduction].

The research presented in PLOS ONE (doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0337914) proposes a fundamentally different approach. Instead of prescribing or manually encoding a universal moral code, the authors argue for an implicit, observationally driven value acquisition paradigm for AI agents. The central hypothesis is that AI can learn a culture's "dynamic values, norms, and behaviors" by being embedded within and observing members of that culture, in a manner analogous to a child assimilating local norms by watching others [Abstract][Introduction]. Here, 'culture' is explicitly defined as a dynamic, cyclical phenomenon, with social groups continuously shaping and adopting new behaviors and expectations.

The technical pivot in this work is the application of inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) to model, infer, and generalize cultural value systems from human behavior. Unlike standard imitation learning—which relies on mimicking demonstrator actions—IRL allows the AI to reverse-engineer the underlying reward function that likely drove these observed decisions. This reward function is sensitive not only to the actions but the context, implicit costs, and benefits, enabling generalization to scenarios outside the training distribution [Introduction][Section 1.2][Abstract].

To empirically validate this framework, the researchers constructed experiments around simple online games encapsulating dimensions of altruism and fairness—core constructs in cross-cultural moral psychology. Participants from different US-based cultural backgrounds, specifically those identifying as White or Latino, were engaged. Prior literature provides robust evidence of systematic cross-group differences: Latinos are characterized as more collectivistic and interdependent, while Whites are more individualistic and autonomy-oriented [Section 1.1]. The experiment thus hypothesized that, even within the same nation, these group-level tendencies would manifest as differentiable patterns in altruistic behavior.

The AI agents, trained via IRL on observation data from these human groups, were able to infer distinct reward functions that reflected the signature altruistic tendencies of each cultural cohort. Notably, the agent could subsequently generalize these learned values to unfamiliar but related contexts—in other words, the internalized value system was applied flexibly beyond the original game scenario [Section 1.2][Abstract]. This recursive generalization is technically significant: it operationalizes how AI might transfer contextualized cultural information, not simply through action mimicry, but by optimizing behavior according to learned, culture-specific reward functions.

This methodology departs methodologically from prior high-profile experiments such as the “Moral Machines” or “Delphi” projects, which elicit explicit value judgments from adult participants faced with stylized moral dilemmas. Instead, the present approach privileges implicit, observational learning akin to ontogenetic human development, suggesting that AI agents can absorb values by watching humans navigate real social trade-offs without explicit instruction or ethical articulation [Section 1.2].

These findings underscore several technical implications for the future of AI value alignment. First, within-culture and between-culture differences in social values—such as altruism and fairness—are learnable by AI agents observing relevant behavioral exemplars. Second, IRL enables not only context-locked value imitation but supports first- and second-order generalization, closely mirroring the adaptive flexibility observed in human moral learning. These properties may prove essential if AI agents are to participate meaningfully and safely within dynamic, pluralistic societies [Abstract][Introduction].

As AI continues to permeate culturally varied human domains, transitioning from static rule application to adaptive, contextually aware behavior will pose new challenges of value alignment, ethical governance, and operational transparency. Technical communities are encouraged to scrutinize the full research dataset and methodological details, which are publicly available at https://github.com/JasmineLi-805/moral-ai-irl, and to engage directly with the open-access article for a comprehensive analysis of experimental protocols and emergent findings.

For expert readers seeking to understand or extend this line of inquiry, the research represents a foundational step toward culturally attuned, observationally grounded AI value systems. Access the full article at https://dx.doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0337914 for complete methodological rigor and data.

Most AI systems today are trained using reinforcement learning, a method that teaches machines what to prioritize by rewarding specific outcomes tied to predefined goals. While effective for narrow tasks, this approach often assumes values are static and universal. As a result, AI systems may struggle to reflect the diverse ways people weigh tradeoffs, cooperate or act altruistically across different cultural contexts.

The researchers then used the data from different human cultural groups to train AI agents, and found the AI agents learned distinct patterns that reflected human group-level differences.

When the AI systems were later tested in a new scenario involving charitable giving, the AI applied what they had learned from the original task. This ability to generalize learned values beyond a single setting suggests that AI systems can learn decision-making tendencies shaped by culture and carry them into next contexts.

title: AI agents debate their way to improved mathematical reasoning
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved impressive results in complex reasoning and knowledge tasks, yet they often struggle with factual accuracy and logical consistency. Prior works have improved LLM performance using prompt-based techniques (e.g. chain-of-thought prompting and self-consistency) and post-hoc self-refinement, but these typically operate on a single model instance. Recently, multi-agent debate frameworks have emerged as a complementary approach, wherein multiple LLM agents propose answers and critique each other’s reasoning to reach consensus. Such a “society of minds” approach has been shown to significantly improve mathematical reasoning and reduce factual hallucinations. However, existing debate methods use homogeneous agents with simple majority voting, limiting their effectiveness. In this work, we propose Adaptive Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Debate (A-HMAD), a novel framework that extends multi-agent debate with (i) diverse specialized agents, (ii) dynamic debate routing, and (iii) a learned consensus mechanism. Each agent in A-HMAD is assigned a distinct role or expertise (e.g. logical reasoning, factual verification, strategic planning), enabling more comprehensive error-checking and perspective diversity than identical agents. A coordination policy dynamically selects which agents contribute at each round based on the question’s domain and the evolving debate state. To aggregate viewpoints, we introduce a consensus optimizer that learns to weight each agent’s vote according to its reliability and the confidence of its arguments. On six challenging benchmarks – including arithmetic QA, grade-school math (GSM8K), multifact question answering (MMLU), factual biography generation, and chess strategy – our A-HMAD consistently outperforms prior single-model methods and the original multi-agent debate baseline. Notably, A-HMAD achieves 4–6% absolute accuracy gains over standard debate on these tasks, and reduces factual errors by over 30% in biography facts. We provide extensive ablations demonstrating the benefits of agent heterogeneity, additional debate rounds, and the learned consensus module. Our findings suggest that an adaptive, role-diverse debating ensemble can drive significant advances in LLM-based educational reasoning, paving the way for safer, more interpretable, and pedagogically reliable AI systems.
introduction: Large-scale pre-trained language models have revolutionized NLP, demonstrating remarkable prowess in tasks requiring knowledge and reasoning (Brown et al. 2020; Srivastava et al. 2023). Models like GPT-3 and its successors exhibit strong few-shot learning abilities (Brown et al. 2020) and can generate coherent multi-step reasoning chains when prompted appropriately (Kojima et al. 2022). Despite these gains, even state-of-the-art LLMs often produce hallucinations – confidently stated false facts – and illogical reasoning leaps, undermining their reliability (Lin et al. 2021; Ji et al. 2023). This is due in part to the noisy, unverified nature of web-scale training data and the lack of explicit reasoning verification in standard next-token prediction training (Ouyang et al. 2022; Ziegler et al. 2019). Improving the factual accuracy and reasoning soundness of LLM outputs has therefore become a pressing research goal (Lee et al. 2022; Kadavath et al. 2022). A rich body of recent work has explored techniques to mitigate LLM reasoning and factual errors. Prompting strategies such as providing step-by-step exemplars or eliciting a chain-of-thought (CoT) have led to notable improvements in arithmetic and commonsense reasoning (Kojima et al. 2022; Wei et al. 2022). For example, Wei et al. (2022) showed that CoT prompting enables models to break down problems into logical steps, and Kojima et al. (2022) found that even zero-shot prompts (e.g. “Let’s think step by step”) can induce multi-step reasoning in LLMs. Building on CoT, self-consistency decoding samples multiple reasoning paths and selects the most common answer, which boosts correctness by averaging out reasoning variance (Wang et al. 2022). Other works equip LLMs with scratchpads or external tools: Nye et al. (2021) introduced “scratchpad” memory for intermediate computations, and tool-use approaches allow models to consult calculators or knowledge bases to check facts (Yao et al. 2023; Schick et al. 2023). Orthogonal to prompting, learning-from-feedback methods like reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) fine-tune models to favor truthful, helpful outputs (Christiano et al. 2017; Stiennon et al. 2020), significantly reducing toxic or untrue generations (Siddiky et al. 2025). Likewise, post-hoc verifier models have been trained to check and correct a model’s answers (e.g. for math word problems Cobbe et al., 2021). Another promising direction is self-reflection and refinement, where the model iteratively critiques and improves its own output (Kawamura et al. 2003; Madaan et al. 2023). Madaan et al. (2023) showed that prompting an LLM to reflect on potential errors and then attempt a revised solution can yield more accurate results, echoing human problem-solving strategies (Renze and Guven 2024). Despite these advances, most techniques above involve a single model reasoning in isolation; the model may still overlook certain errors or fail to verify all facts due to its inherent biases or blind spots (Kadavath et al. 2022; Wei et al. 2022). In contrast to single-model approaches, multi-agent frameworks draw inspiration from social or argumentative processes in which multiple experts collaborate or debate to reach a better answer (Michael et al. 2023). Early conceptual work by Minsky posited that intelligent behavior could emerge from a “Society of Mind” – a diverse collection of simpler agents. In the context of AI alignment and truthfulness, Khan et al. (2024) proposed AI debate, where two agents argue for opposing answers and a judge (human or AI) decides which argument is more convincing. The hope is that through adversarial debate, the truth would prevail because any false argument can be revealed by the opponent (Khan et al. 2024). While this work was theoretical, recent empirical studies have started to realize the benefits of multi-agent interactions. For example, Li et al. (2023) introduced a communicative multi-agent system (Camel) where agents with different roles (user, assistant, critic) collaborate to solve tasks. Similarly, MetaGPT by Hong et al. (2023) uses multiple specialized agent personas (e.g. PM, engineer, tester) to decompose and solve complex programming tasks, showing the power of role specialization. Focusing on language tasks, Kadavath et al. (2022) demonstrated that allowing two LLMs to debate from different viewpoints encourages divergent thinking and can improve answer quality. Parrish et al. (2022) found that when a less truthful LLM “debates” a more truthful LLM, the resulting answers tend to be more factual, suggesting that persuasion by a knowledgeable agent can correct the other’s misconceptions. These works hint that multi-agent interaction might overcome individual model limitations. However, some studies report mixed results; Chan et al. (2023) showed that a simple two-turn debate did not significantly help humans answer reading comprehension questions, implying that how debate is structured is critical. Most relevant to our work, Du et al. (2023) recently proposed a multi-agent debate framework for LLMs to improve both reasoning and factuality. In their approach, multiple instances of the same LLM are each prompted to generate an answer with reasoning. The agents then exchange their answers and critique each other’s reasoning over several rounds, before outputting a final consensus answer. This method, requiring only black-box access to the models’ outputs, was shown to significantly enhance performance on math word problems, strategic games, and factual QA, compared to single-agent baselines. Notably, Du et al. reported that debate reduces hallucinations: agents often identify and remove one another’s uncertain or inconsistent facts, leading to a final answer with more reliable information. The multi-agent debate can be seen as inducing an ensemble-like effect where multiple reasoning paths are explored in parallel and vetted against each other, yielding higher confidence when they converge. Zhang et al. (2023) further applied a debating agents idea for LLM-based evaluation, showing that a multi-agent debate can refine judgment of answers. Wu et al. (2024) used debates between a “student” and “professor” model to supervise and improve unreliable reasoning, while Zelikman et al. (2022) leveraged cooperative agents for embodied decision-making. These underline a growing consensus that multi-agent interactions can enhance an LLM’s performance and reliability. Despite the promise of multi-agent debate, there remain clear opportunities for improvement. First, prior debate frameworks largely use homogeneous agents – e.g. identical GPT-3.5 models with the same prompting style. This limits the diversity of perspectives; if all agents share similar strengths and weaknesses, certain errors may go unchallenged. Recent works in multi-agent collaboration emphasize heterogeneity as a key factor for success (Li et al. 2023; Hong et al. 2023), which suggests that introducing specialized agents could further improve the debate outcome. Second, existing methods rely on a simple majority vote or unanimous agreement for the final answer. This treats all agents as equally reliable, ignoring the possibility that some agents (or certain argument lines) might be more likely correct than others. A more nuanced consensus mechanism that weights agents by their past accuracy or the confidence of their justifications could lead to more robust final answers. Third, the debate protocol in prior work is static – agents alternate in a fixed order and number of rounds. In complex scenarios, a rigid protocol might be suboptimal: for example, if a particular agent has expertise most relevant to the query, the framework should allocate more opportunity for that agent to contribute, while bypassing irrelevant agents to save computation. Such dynamic agent routing or adaptive scheduling of debate rounds has not been explored in existing work. Finally, no prior study (to our knowledge) has integrated an explicit learning component into the debate loop – the interplay is handled by prompting alone. This raises the question of whether parts of the multi-agent debate process (e.g. how to aggregate answers) can be learned from data to further boost performance. In this paper, we propose a new framework called Adaptive Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Debate (A-HMAD) that addresses the above limitations and substantially improves LLM factuality and reasoning via multi-agent debate. Our contributions are summarized as follows: (1) We design a heterogeneous agent ensemble where each agent has a specialized role or distinct model type, along with a dynamic routing strategy that activates different subsets of agents depending on the query type and intermediate debate outcomes. (2) We propose a learned consensus module that replaces simple majority voting for final answer selection, weighting agents based on features of their responses and confidence. (3) We thoroughly evaluate A-HMAD on six challenging benchmarks, achieving new state-of-the-art results for our base model, and significantly reducing hallucinations and incorrect facts compared to single-agent and prior debate baselines. (4) Through controlled ablations and qualitative case studies, we show why our components matter and how expert agents can correct others’ errors. Together, these advances move us closer to robust, truth-seeking AI systems that can reliably solve complex problems and self-correct errors.

A-HMAD’s operational structure redefines the debate framework: each agent is either tasked with dedicated responsibilities (such as logical reasoning, factual verification, or strategic planning), or constructed from a distinct architectural variant. The framework deploys dynamic routing, meaning that agent participation is not static but instead contextually determined by domain relevance and the ongoing debate state. As the boundaries of LLM application expand, the necessity for architectures that can systematically challenge, correct, and corroborate internal reasoning chains becomes ever more pressing. The Adaptive Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Debate framework establishes a technically substantiated benchmark for such systems. For researchers and practitioners intent on deploying reliable, trustworthy AI, a granular engagement with A-HMAD’s methodology and empirical findings is essential.

Read the full research paper for technical details and empirical benchmarks: [https://dx.doi.org/10.1007/s44443-025-00353-3](https://dx.doi.org/10.1007/s44443-025-00353-3)

title: Periodic table for AI methods aims to drive innovation
abstract:
Deep Variational Multivariate Information Bottleneck - A Framework for Variational Losses

Abstract Variational dimensionality reduction methods are widely used for their accuracy, generative capabil- ities, and robustness. We introduce a unifying framework that generalizes both such as traditional and state-of-the-art methods. The framework is based on an interpretation of the multivariate information bottleneck, trading off the information preserved in an encoder graph (defining what to compress) against that in a decoder graph (defining a generative model for data). Using this approach, we rederive existing methods, including the deep variational information bottleneck, variational autoencoders, and deep multiview information bottleneck. We naturally extend the deep variational CCA (DVCCA) family to beta-DVCCA and introduce a new method, the deep variational symmetric information bottleneck (DVSIB). DSIB, the deterministic limit of DVSIB, connects to modern contrastive learning approaches such as Barlow Twins, among others. We evaluate these methods on Noisy MNIST and Noisy CIFAR-100, showing that algorithms better matched to the structure of the problem like DVSIB and beta-DVCCA produce better latent spaces as measured by classification accuracy, dimensionality of the latent variables, sample efficiency, and consistently outperform other approaches under comparable conditions. Additionally, we benchmark against state-of-the-art models, achieving superior or competitive accuracy. Our results demonstrate that this framework can seamlessly incorporate diverse multi-view representation learning algorithms, providing a foundation for designing novel, problem-specific loss functions. Keywords: Generative Models, Dimensionality Reduction, Data Efficiency
introduction:
1 Introduction

Large dimensional multi-modal datasets are abundant in multimedia systems utilized for language modeling (Xu et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2018; Rohrbach et al., 2017; Sanabria et al., 2018; Goyal et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2018; Hendrycks et al., 2020), neural control of behavior studies (Steinmetz et al., 2021; Urai et al., 2022; Krakauer et al., 2017; Pang et al., 2016), multi-omics approaches in systems biology (Clark et al., 2013; Zheng et al., 2017; Svensson et al., 2018; Huntley et al., 2015; Lorenzi et al., 2018), and many other domains. Such data come with the curse of dimensionality, making it hard to learn the relevant statistical correlations from samples. The problem is made even harder by the data often containing information that is irrelevant to the specific questions one asks. To tackle these challenges, a myriad of supervised and unsupervised dimensionality reduction (DR) methods have emerged. By preserving certain aspects of the data while discarding the remainder, DR can decrease the complexity of the problem, yield clearer insights, and provide a foundation for more refined modeling approaches.

DR techniques span linear methods like Principal Component Analysis (PCA) (Hotelling, 1933), Partial Least Squares (PLS) (Wold et al., 2001), Canonical Correlations Analysis (CCA) (Hotelling, 1936), and regularized CCA (Vinod, 1976; Årup Nielsen et al., 1998), as well as nonlinear approaches, including Autoencoders (AE) (Hinton and Salakhutdinov, 2006), Deep CCA (Andrew et al., 2013), Deep Canonical Correlated AE (Wang et al., 2015), Correlational Neural Networks (Chandar et al., 2016), Deep Generalized CCA (Benton et al., 2017), and Deep Tensor CCA (Wong et al., 2021). Of particular interest to us are variational methods, such as Variational Autoencoders (VAE) (Kingma and Welling, 2014), beta-VAE (Higgins et al., 2016), Joint Multimodal VAE (JMVAE) (Suzuki et al., 2016), Deep Variational CCA (DVCCA) (Wang et al., 2016), Deep Variational Information Bottleneck (DVIB) (Alemi et al., 2017), Variational Mixture-of-experts AE (Shi et al., 2019), and Multiview Information Bottleneck (Federici et al., 2020b). These DR methods use deep neural networks and variational approximations to learn robust and accurate representations of the data, while, at the same time, often serving as generative models for creating samples from the learned distributions.

There are many theoretical derivations and justifications for variational DR methods (Kingma and Welling, 2014; Higgins et al., 2016; Suzuki et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2016; Karami and Schuurmans, 2021; Qiu et al., 2022; Alemi et al., 2017; Bao, 2021; Lee and Van der Schaar, 2021; Wang et al., 2019; Wan et al., 2021; Federici et al., 2020a; Huang et al., 2022; Hu et al., 2020). This diversity of derivations, while enabling adaptability, often leaves researchers with no principled ways for choosing a method for a particular application, for designing new methods with distinct assumptions, or for comparing methods to each other.

Here, we introduce the Deep Variational Multivariate Information Bottleneck (DVMIB) framework, offering a unified mathematical foundation for many variational—and deterministic—DR methods. Our framework is grounded in the multivariate information bottleneck loss function (Tishby et al., 2000; Friedman et al., 2013). This loss, amenable to approximation through upper and lower variational bounds, provides a system for implementing diverse DR variants using deep neural networks. We demonstrate the framework’s efficacy by deriving the loss functions of many existing DR methods starting from the same principles. These include well-known methods, such as AEs (Hinton and Salakhutdinov, 2006), VAEs (Kingma and Welling, 2014), and state-of-the-art methods such as Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining (CLIP) (Radford et al., 2021) and Barlow Twins (Zbontar et al., 2021). Furthermore, our framework naturally allows the adjustment of trade-off parameters, leading to generalizations of these existing methods. For instance, we generalize DVCCA (Wang et al., 2016) to β-DVCCA. The framework further allows us to introduce and implement in software novel DR methods. We view the DVMIB framework, with its uniform information bottleneck language, conceptual clarity of translating statistical dependencies in data via graphical models of encoder and decoder structures into variational losses, the ability to unify existing approaches, and easy adaptability to new scenarios as one of the main contributions of our work.

Beyond its unifying role, our framework offers a principled approach for deriving problem-specific loss functions using domain-specific knowledge. Thus, we anticipate its application for multi-view representation learning across diverse fields. To illustrate this, we use the framework to derive a novel dimensionality reduction method, the Deep Variational Symmetric Information Bottleneck (DVSIB), which compresses two random variables into two distinct latent variables that are maximally informative about one another. This new method produces better representations of classic datasets than previous approaches. The introduction of DVSIB is another major contribution of our paper.

In summary, our paper makes the following contributions to the field:

1. Introduction of the Variational Multivariate Information Bottleneck Framework: We provide both intuitive and mathematical insights into this framework, establishing a robust foundation for further exploration.

2. Rederivation and Generalization of Existing Methods within a Common Framework: We demonstrate the versatility of our framework by systematically rederiving and generalizing various existing methods from the literature, showcasing the framework’s ability to unify diverse approaches.

3. Design of a Novel Method — Deep Variational Symmetric Information Bottleneck (DVSIB): Employing our framework, we introduce DVSIB as a new method, contributing to the growing repertoire of techniques in variational dimensionality reduction. The method constructs high-accuracy latent spaces from substantially fewer samples than comparable approaches. Additionally, its deterministic version, the Deterministic Symmetric Information Bottleneck (DSIB), can be mapped to a plethora of state-of-the-art methods including CLIP and Barlow Twins.

The paper is structured as follows: First, in Sec. 2, we introduce the underlying mathematics and an implementation of the DVMIB framework. Then, in Sec. 3, we explain how to use the framework to generate new DR methods. In this section, in Tbl. 1, we present several known and newly variational DR methods, illustrating how easily they can be derived within the framework. In the Results (Sec. 4), as a proof of concept, we first benchmark simple computational implementations of the methods in Tbl. 1 against the Noisy MNIST dataset (Sec. 4.1). We then examine whether the trends observed in Noisy MNIST hold for more complex datasets, i.e., Noisy CIFAR-100, and more complex neural network architectures, namely CNNs (Sec. 4.2). Next, we demonstrate how the framework extends to more advanced, state-of-the-art methods (Sec. 4.3). The Appendices provide a detailed treatment of all terms in the different loss functions introduced (Appx. A). Appendix B.2 discusses auxiliary private variable models. Additional details, including visualizations, and a comprehensive performance analysis of many methods on Noisy MNIST are available in Appx. C. Further results for Noisy CIFAR-100 are provided in Appx. D.
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline: Unifying AI’s Core Tools: New Information Bottleneck Framework Reshapes Dimensionality Reduction

News Article:

A recent seminar in the Machine Learning Seminar Series Spring 2026 spotlighted a unified variational information-theoretic framework, demonstrating that several leading approaches to out-of-distribution detection could be interpreted, analyzed, and advanced within a single mathematical structure. This event underscores a larger wave of research, exemplified by the Deep Variational Multivariate Information Bottleneck (DVMIB) framework, which consolidates and generalizes a wide range of variational dimensionality reduction (DR) methods under a principled, information-theoretic lens ([Citation]).

Dimensionality reduction plays a foundational role in machine learning and data science, especially as datasets scale in size, complexity, and modality. Whether in language modeling, multimedia systems, behavioral neuroscience, or multi-omics biology, practitioners face the intensive challenge of extracting meaningful patterns from data bedeviled by high dimensionality and irrelevant variance ([Introduction]). Historically, a host of techniques—from Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and Canonical Correlations Analysis (CCA) to nonlinear autoencoders and deep neural models—have attempted to address this issue, each with its own assumptions and limitations.

The DVMIB framework presents a unified mathematical foundation for both classical and state-of-the-art variational DR approaches. Rooted in the multivariate information bottleneck principle, DVMIB operationalizes a crucial trade-off: it defines, via an encoder graph, what information to compress from the data, and, through a decoder graph, what generative structure to preserve ([Abstract], [Introduction]). This allows practitioners to formally rederive and rigorously compare a spectrum of existing DR methods—including deep variational information bottleneck models, variational autoencoders (VAE), deep canonical correlation analysis (CCA), and contemporary contrastive approaches like CLIP and Barlow Twins—all from common statistical principles ([Abstract]).

Key to DVMIB’s significance is its generalization power. Not only does the framework recover the loss functions of established methods, but it systematically extends their capabilities. For example, it introduces the deep variational symmetric information bottleneck (DVSIB): a new method that compresses two random variables into distinct latent representations that maximize shared information ([Introduction]). The deterministic version, DSIB, bridges to recent contrastive learning paradigms, enabling connections to high-performing methods such as Barlow Twins ([Abstract]).

Empirical evaluations reinforce these theoretical advances. Applying DVMIB and its variants to standard benchmarks like Noisy MNIST and Noisy CIFAR-100, the authors report superior or competitive classification accuracy, lower latent dimensionality, and enhanced sample efficiency—demonstrating the practical gains from matching algorithms to the intrinsic structure of the problem ([Abstract]). The framework’s adaptability also extends to integration with more advanced architectures, facilitating benchmarks against complex neural networks such as CNNs ([Introduction]).

Beyond technical performance, DVMIB directly addresses the proliferation of DR algorithms and their often-disparate derivations. Traditional approaches have left researchers without principled tools for method selection, comparison, or the design of novel loss functions. By casting all these options within a uniform information bottleneck language—grounded in graphical models of encoders and decoders—DVMIB brings conceptual clarity and practical modularity to both analysis and deployment across research domains ([Introduction]).

A central contribution is DVMIB's utility as a foundation for problem-specific algorithmic innovation. By allowing fine-grained adjustment of trade-off parameters within its loss function construction, the framework enables researchers to derive new DR variants tailored to the statistical dependencies and requirements of their application ([Introduction]). The work ultimately provides extensible software and mathematical foundations to enable straightforward experimentation and adaptation—qualities highlighted in proof-of-concept derivations and benchmarking experiments ([Abstract]).

The ongoing integration of such unified frameworks with real-world machine learning practices, as reflected in recent seminar series, signals an evolving methodological landscape—where new frontiers in data efficiency and representation learning will be built not from isolated advances, but from robust mathematical foundations serving as common denominators.

For technical readers and domain experts seeking rigorous, extensible tools for multi-view representation learning or data-driven modeling, the full paper offers detailed mathematical formulations, derivations, and experimental results. Access the research at https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2310.03311 to explore the framework’s breadth, implementations, and implications for next-generation machine learning systems.

anchor_event.summary: Result [4] from the Machine Learning Seminar Series Spring 2026 announces research on a unified variational information-theoretic framework, including demonstrations that it outperforms state-of-the-art methods. This closely mirrors the unifying variational dimensionality reduction (DR) approach of the primary paper and provides high technical depth and source quality—ideal as a hook to introduce novel frameworks making measurable progress over existing techniques.
anchor_event.full_content:
This talk focuses on out-of-distribution (OOD) detection, a key capability for safe and reliable AI. The first part presents a mathematical theory of OOD detection that places state-of-the-art methods, largely heuristically derived, within a unified variational information-theoretic framework [1]. The theory provides plausible assumptions behind existing approaches and predicts new OOD detectors that are simple to implement and outperform state-of-the-art methods.

The second part of the talk addresses an often overlooked problem in practical deployment of OOD detectors, that is, OOD detectors depend on parameters that must be tuned, and a “given OOD” dataset is required [2]. In practice, such given OOD data may be difficult to obtain. We formalize this problem and introduce a new tuning strategy that uses only the model’s training data and achieves similar or better performance compared to tuning on given OOD data, enabling robust and practical deployment.

**Abstract:**Modern machine learning systems achieve impressive performance, but are largely built under a closed-world assumption: that the data distribution does not change from the distribution of the training set. Real environments are open, dynamic, and filled with unknown unknowns. In such settings, knowing when a model’s output is reliable is critical.
